proposals.title: Unified Self-Supervised Pretraining for Multi-Modal Medical Imaging and Multi-Task Diagnosis
proposals.motivation: Medical AI models are typically trained for single tasks on single imaging modalities (e.g., chest X-rays), limiting their clinical utility. Real-world diagnosis requires synthesizing information from multiple modalities (e.g., CT, MRI, Ultrasound) and performing a suite of diagnostic tasks. Current self-supervised learning (SSL) frameworks, while powerful for scaling, are not designed to efficiently learn joint representations from inherently heterogeneous multi-modal medical data and transfer to diverse downstream tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: A unified SSL framework, trained on a large-scale dataset of paired multi-modal medical images, can learn robust, modality-agnostic representations that enable superior performance and data efficiency on a diverse set of diagnostic tasks compared to modality-specific or task-specific pretraining.
proposals.method: We will develop a cross-modal contrastive SSL architecture, extending principles from models like CLIP and MoCo. The model will use a dual-encoder design with modality-specific backbones (e.g., 3D ConvNets for CT, Vision Transformers for fundus images) projecting into a shared latent space. We will design novel pretext tasks including cross-modal instance discrimination, masked autoencoding across modalities, and modality-invariant feature learning via adversarial training. This model will be pretrained on a newly curated dataset of paired studies (e.g., chest CT with corresponding X-ray and clinical notes).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a scalable SSL framework for heterogeneous medical imaging data, 2) a new benchmark dataset for multi-modal medical SSL, and 3) empirical evidence demonstrating that unified multi-modal pretraining outperforms established single-modality SSL (e.g., SimCLR, MAE) on a battery of diagnostic tasks including classification, segmentation, and severity scoring.
proposals.novelty: While multi-modal SSL exists in general vision, its application to the specific challenges of aligned but heterogeneous medical imaging (different resolutions, contrasts, anatomical coverage) is unexplored. The proposed framework uniquely integrates cross-modal contrastive learning with task-agnostic feature learning specifically for clinical multi-task transfer.

proposals.title: MedCLIP: Scaling Vision-Language Self-Supervision for General-Purpose Medical Image Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Current medical vision models are typically trained on narrow, task-specific datasets, limiting their generalization and data efficiency. The success of contrastive vision-language pretraining (e.g., CLIP) in natural images demonstrates the power of learning from rich, web-scale image-text pairs, but this paradigm has not been fully realized in medicine due to the lack of a large, curated, paired dataset and domain-specific adaptation. Creating a foundational medical vision-language model could enable zero-shot diagnosis, improve data efficiency for downstream tasks, and provide interpretable model reasoning through language.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a vision-language model, pretrained at scale on a novel dataset of medical images paired with their corresponding radiology reports or figure captions, will learn robust, general-purpose visual representations that outperform supervised and self-supervised baselines on diverse diagnostic tasks, particularly in low-data regimes.
proposals.method: We will construct a large-scale dataset of medical images (e.g., chest X-rays, CTs, MRIs) paired with textual descriptions from public sources like MIMIC-CXR and PubMed Central. We will adapt the CLIP framework, using a Vision Transformer (ViT) as the image encoder and a Transformer-based text encoder. The model will be trained with a contrastive loss (InfoNCE) to maximize the similarity between an image and its correct report/caption while minimizing similarity to mismatched pairs. We will systematically scale the dataset size and model parameters to study scaling laws. The learned representations will be evaluated via linear probing, fine-tuning, and zero-shot transfer on established benchmarks like CheXpert and NIH ChestX-ray14.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a new large-scale, public dataset of paired medical image-text data; 2) a scalable, open-source vision-language model (MedCLIP) for medical imaging; and 3) a comprehensive benchmark demonstrating the advantages of this approach for data-efficient learning and zero-shot generalization across multiple imaging modalities and clinical tasks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has used text for medical image classification, this proposal focuses on scaling a contrastive vision-language pretraining paradigm, akin to CLIP, specifically for the medical domain, which is novel in its ambition to create a general-purpose foundation model from heterogeneous medical literature data rather than task-specific fine-tuning.

proposals.title: Supervised Learning with Self-Supervised Inductive Biases: A Data-Efficient Framework for Medical Vision
proposals.motivation: Self-supervised learning (SSL) has demonstrated remarkable data efficiency by learning robust representations from unlabeled data, but its final performance often requires fine-tuning on labeled data. In medical imaging, labeled datasets are scarce and expensive to curate, creating a critical need for supervised methods that can match SSL's sample efficiency. This work aims to bridge this gap by directly instilling SSL's powerful inductive biases into supervised training paradigms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly incorporating the geometric and semantic consistency constraints central to contrastive and masked image modeling SSL methods into a supervised loss framework will yield models that achieve high accuracy with significantly fewer labeled medical images than standard supervised training.
proposals.method: We will develop a dual-objective training framework for vision transformers (ViTs) where the primary supervised loss (e.g., cross-entropy for disease classification) is jointly optimized with an auxiliary SSL-style loss. Specifically, we will integrate a contrastive loss (inspired by SimCLR) that enforces invariance to clinically meaningful augmentations (e.g., controlled intensity shifts, minor rotations) and a reconstruction loss from masked autoencoding (MAE) to encourage learning of anatomical structure. The model will be pre-trained on a large-scale, unlabeled chest CT dataset (e.g., a subset of NIH DeepLesion) and then fine-tuned with our combined loss on small, labeled target tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, practical training recipe for data-efficient supervised learning in medical computer vision, validated on benchmarks like CheXpert and RSNA Pneumonia Detection. It will provide empirical evidence and theoretical analysis on how SSL mechanisms can be distilled for supervised efficiency, offering a path to high performance in label-scarce clinical settings.
proposals.novelty: While SSL is used for pre-training, the novelty lies in the concurrent, synergistic use of SSL objectives *during* supervised fine-tuning on limited labels, explicitly shaping the optimization landscape rather than just providing initialization. This approach is distinct from prior works that treat SSL pre-training and supervised learning as separate, sequential phases.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Video Pretraining for Dynamic Phenotype Learning in Chest CT
proposals.motivation: Current medical vision models are predominantly trained on static, single-snapshot images, ignoring the rich temporal dynamics inherent in patient care. This limits their ability to model disease progression, treatment response, and longitudinal risk. Building on the success of video pretraining in non-medical domains and its recent promise in 3D CT, a dedicated framework for learning from temporal sequences of medical images is a critical unmet need.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pretraining a vision model on longitudinal sequences of chest CT scans, using a self-supervised objective designed for temporal consistency, will yield representations that significantly outperform static-image models on tasks requiring an understanding of disease dynamics, such as predicting future exacerbation of interstitial lung disease.
proposals.method: We will curate a large-scale longitudinal dataset of de-identified chest CT series from the same patients over time. The model will be a 3D convolutional network (e.g., a Video Swin Transformer) pretrained using a novel masked temporal modeling objective, where spans of 3D volumes across time are masked and the model must reconstruct them using spatiotemporal context. This will be combined with a contrastive loss that enforces similarity between representations of the same patient's scans at different time points versus those of other patients. The pretrained model will be fine-tuned on specific prognostic tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel pretraining paradigm and model for longitudinal medical imaging, 2) a benchmark dataset and tasks for evaluating temporal understanding in chest CT, and 3) empirical evidence that temporal pretraining unlocks new capabilities for predicting clinical trajectories, moving beyond static diagnosis.
proposals.novelty: While video pretraining exists for natural scenes and has been applied to single 3D medical volumes, its adaptation to learn from *irregularly sampled longitudinal series* of 3D scans for dynamic phenotype learning is novel. This directly relaxes the static-image constraint pervasive in medical AI.

proposals.title: GLoRIA-3D: Learning Aligned 3D Medical Image Representations from Paired Volumes and Text
proposals.motivation: Learning robust, data-efficient representations for 3D medical imaging (e.g., CT, MRI) is critical for advancing automated diagnosis and quantitative analysis. Current methods often rely on large, task-specific labeled datasets, which are scarce. Vision-language pretraining frameworks like GLoRIA have shown success in 2D by learning from paired images and text, but extending this to 3D data remains an open challenge with significant potential for label-efficient learning across diverse volumetric imaging tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extending the GLoRIA framework to 3D volumetric data will enable the learning of semantically rich, general-purpose 3D visual representations from paired radiology reports, and that these representations will outperform supervised models trained from scratch on downstream tasks with limited labels.
proposals.method: We will adapt the GLoRIA (GLObal-Local Representation Learning via Language-guided Attention) framework for 3D. A 3D CNN (e.g., a 3D ResNet) or a vision transformer adapted for volumetric patches will serve as the image encoder. The text encoder will process paired radiology reports. The core GLoRIA attention mechanism will be extended to compute local similarity between 3D feature sub-volumes and text token embeddings, in addition to global image-text contrastive loss. We will pretrain on large-scale datasets of paired 3D medical scans and reports (e.g., MIMIC-CXR-3D or a curated collection of CT studies).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) the first open-source, extensible framework for 3D medical vision-language pretraining based on GLoRIA principles, 2) a set of pretrained 3D visual encoders for the community, and 3) empirical evidence demonstrating improved label efficiency on key downstream 3D tasks like organ segmentation, disease classification, and lesion detection compared to supervised baselines.
proposals.novelty: While 3D self-supervised learning (e.g., via contrastive or reconstruction) exists, applying a fine-grained, language-guided attention mechanism like GLoRIA's to 3D medical data is novel. It moves beyond global image-text alignment to capture localized, semantically meaningful 3D structures described in text, which is non-trivial given the computational and architectural challenges of 3D.

proposals.title: Genomic-Vision-Language Pretraining for Precision Oncology: A Multimodal Framework for Outcome Prediction
proposals.motivation: Current multimodal models in medical AI primarily fuse imaging and clinical text, omitting the foundational molecular drivers of disease like genomic alterations. In oncology, this limits the ability to predict patient-specific outcomes and therapeutic responses, which are governed by a complex interplay of imaging phenotypes and genomic profiles. Integrating genomics with vision and language is a critical, unsolved challenge for building truly holistic diagnostic and prognostic AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a transformer-based architecture, pretrained on aligned histopathology images, clinical reports, and sparse genomic features (e.g., mutational status, gene expression signatures), will learn a more powerful and biologically grounded representation, leading to superior performance on tasks like survival prediction and therapy recommendation compared to models using any modality subset.
proposals.method: We will develop a tri-modal encoder architecture. A vision transformer (ViT) will process whole-slide histopathology image tiles, a clinical BERT will process pathology reports, and a lightweight dense network will encode structured genomic vectors. Drawing from recent video-language pretraining methods, we will employ a cross-modal attention fusion module to align representations, followed by a shared multimodal transformer for joint reasoning. We will leverage large-scale, linkable datasets like The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA), which provides paired WSIs, clinical notes, and genomic data, for masked-modality modeling and contrastive pretraining.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, scalable framework for integrating high-dimensional genomic data with vision-language models in medicine. It will establish new benchmarks for multimodal fusion in computational pathology and provide a pretrained model for the research community. The resulting system will offer a significant step towards AI that can reason across the phenotypic, narrative, and molecular axes of cancer.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has fused images and text, and some studies have combined images with genomics, the simultaneous integration of vision, language, and genomics into a single pretrained transformer framework is novel. The approach innovates by adapting video-language fusion techniques to the distinct challenge of aligning a dense, spatially-rich modality (images) with a non-spatial, high-dimensional modality (genomics) and a sequential one (language).

proposals.title: Video-Pretrained Foundation Models for Longitudinal Prognosis and Treatment Outcome Forecasting in Chest CT
proposals.motivation: Current deep learning models for clinical prediction often require task-specific training on large, labeled datasets, which are scarce for complex longitudinal outcomes like disease progression or treatment response. Building on the success of video pretraining for 3D anatomical understanding, there is a significant opportunity to leverage these learned spatiotemporal representations for high-stakes prognostic tasks, moving beyond diagnostic classification to predictive, patient-specific forecasting.
proposals.hypothesis: Spatiotemporal visual representations learned from large-scale video pretraining on diverse chest CT sequences are transferable and superior to representations from static image pretraining for forecasting longitudinal clinical outcomes, such as survival risk and complication likelihood following intervention.
proposals.method: We will adapt a video-pretrained transformer model (e.g., a TimeSformer or Video Swin Transformer backbone) as a frozen feature extractor. Using a curated longitudinal chest CT dataset (e.g., NLST, LIDC-IDRI with outcome metadata), we will train lightweight prediction heads (e.g., transformer decoders or MLPs) on top of the extracted spatiotemporal features. The core technical approach involves formulating prognosis as a sequence-to-sequence or time-to-event (Cox regression) prediction task, where the model ingests a baseline CT scan and optionally a follow-up scan to forecast a trajectory of future risk scores or discrete outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a new paradigm for medical prognosis using general-purpose video foundation models, reducing the need for massive, outcome-specific annotated data. It will provide a benchmark for transferable spatiotemporal representations in clinical forecasting and release code and model weights adapted for longitudinal analysis.
proposals.novelty: While video pretraining has shown promise for 3D diagnostic tasks, its application to longitudinal outcome forecasting is unexplored. The novelty lies in repurposing dynamics-learned representations, typically for action recognition, to model the implicit temporal evolution of disease for future-state prediction.

proposals.title: MED-CLIP: Efficient Adaptation of General Vision-Language Models for Medical Image-Text Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Medical AI suffers from data scarcity and high annotation costs. While large-scale vision-language models (VLMs) like CLIP have shown remarkable zero-shot capabilities in the general domain, their direct application to medical imaging is suboptimal due to the domain gap in visual features and specialized terminology. Adapting these powerful pretrained models efficiently could unlock robust, data-efficient medical image understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a targeted, parameter-efficient fine-tuning strategy applied to a general-domain VLM can create a medical-aligned representation space that significantly outperforms both the original model and models trained from scratch on limited medical data for downstream diagnostic and retrieval tasks.
proposals.method: We will adapt a CLIP-style architecture (ViT-B/16 image encoder, Transformer text encoder) pretrained on web-scale data. Instead of full fine-tuning, we will employ parameter-efficient methods like visual prompt tuning (VPT) for the image encoder and low-rank adaptation (LoRA) for the text encoder, using a curated dataset of paired medical images (e.g., chest X-rays, retinal scans) and their corresponding radiology reports or captions. The model will be trained with a contrastive loss to align image and report embeddings, followed by evaluation on zero-shot classification, cross-modal retrieval, and fine-tuned performance on specific diagnostic tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a publicly available, efficiently tuned medical VLM benchmark (MED-CLIP) and a systematic analysis of adaptation strategies for VLMs in medicine. It will demonstrate a practical path to leveraging general-domain pretraining for medical AI, reducing data needs and improving model generalization across multiple imaging modalities and tasks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has fine-tuned VLMs for medicine, our approach systematically explores and benchmarks parameter-efficient adaptation techniques specifically for this domain, focusing on preserving general knowledge while acquiring medical expertise—a non-trivial optimization given the scale of base models and the specificity of the target domain.

proposals.title: UniMed: Unified Self-Supervision via Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining and Masked Image Modeling for Medical Vision
proposals.motivation: Current medical vision models rely heavily on scarce, expert-labeled data, limiting their scalability and robustness. While self-supervised learning (SSL) from computer vision offers a path forward, methods like contrastive image-text learning (e.g., CLIP) and intra-modal SSL (e.g., Masked Image Modeling, MIM) have complementary strengths but are typically explored in isolation. A unified framework is needed to combine the rich semantic alignment of language with the dense, contextual feature learning of MIM for complex medical domains.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that jointly training a vision encoder with a contrastive image-text objective and a masked image modeling objective will yield representations that are more semantically grounded, anatomically precise, and data-efficient for downstream medical tasks than models trained with either objective alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a dual-branch transformer architecture where a shared vision encoder (e.g., ViT) receives two views of a medical image (e.g., chest X-ray or CT slice). One branch performs Masked Image Modeling (MAE-style) to reconstruct randomly masked patches. The other branch aligns the global image representation with a paired radiology report text embedding via a contrastive loss (CLIP-style). The model will be pretrained at scale on large, unlabeled medical image-text datasets like MIMIC-CXR, with careful balancing of the two loss terms.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new, powerful pretraining paradigm for medical AI that reduces dependency on fine-grained labels. We will deliver a publicly available model and benchmark its performance on diverse downstream tasks including classification (CheXpert), segmentation (NIH DeepLesion), and visual question answering (VQA-RAD), demonstrating superior data efficiency and generalization.
proposals.novelty: While CLIP and MAE are established in natural images, their synergistic integration for medical vision is unexplored. This proposal uniquely combines inter-modal (image-text) and intra-modal (image-only) self-supervision within a single medical pretraining framework, moving beyond simple adaptation of natural image models.

proposals.title: MedPrompt: Prompt-Based Meta-Learning for Few-Shot Adaptation of Vision-Language Models to Novel Medical Imaging Tasks
proposals.motivation: In medical AI, acquiring large, annotated datasets for every new diagnostic task is a major bottleneck, limiting the deployment of models in specialized or low-resource settings. While large-scale pretrained vision-language models (VLMs) like CLIP show promise for zero-shot transfer, their performance on complex, domain-specific medical tasks without fine-tuning remains suboptimal. Adapting efficient few-shot learning techniques from NLP, such as prompt tuning and meta-learning, could enable rapid, data-efficient customization of these models for novel clinical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learning framework for learning to generate or optimize continuous prompt embeddings for a medical vision-language model will significantly outperform standard zero-shot and linear-probe few-shot baselines on novel diagnostic imaging tasks with minimal labeled examples.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) framework to optimize the initialization of continuous prompt vectors for a frozen, large-scale VLM (e.g., a medically-pretrained CLIP variant). The inner-loop will perform few-shot adaptation on a support set of novel medical tasks (e.g., rare disease classification from chest CT), updating only the prompt parameters. The outer-loop will meta-learn an initial prompt representation that generalizes across tasks. We will benchmark against standard zero-shot prompting, linear probing, and black-box prompt engineering on a curated suite of few-shot medical vision tasks derived from public datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical method for data-efficient adaptation of large VLMs to new medical imaging tasks, reducing reliance on exhaustive annotation. It will provide empirical evidence on the transferability of NLP-inspired few-shot techniques to the medical vision domain and release a benchmark for evaluating few-shot medical vision adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While prompt tuning and meta-learning are established in NLP, their joint application for adapting vision-language models to the structured, high-stakes domain of medical imaging with explicit few-shot benchmarks is novel. This bridges a gap between general-purpose vision-language pretraining and the precise, data-scarce needs of clinical task adaptation.

proposals.title: Uncertainty-Aware Multimodal Pretraining: Quantifying Epistemic and Aleatoric Uncertainty for Robust Medical Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: In high-stakes medical vision tasks, such as chest CT analysis, deep learning models often produce overconfident predictions without reliable uncertainty estimates, limiting clinical trust and safe deployment. Pre-2026, large-scale pretraining on multimodal datasets (image-text) has improved performance but rarely provides calibrated uncertainty for both predictions and the learned multimodal representations themselves. This gap is critical for identifying model failures on out-of-distribution data or ambiguous cases.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating Bayesian deep learning principles into multimodal pretraining frameworks will yield models that provide well-calibrated, disentangled estimates of epistemic (model) and aleatoric (data) uncertainty, and that this uncertainty will correlate with diagnostic difficulty and out-of-distribution detection in downstream medical tasks.
proposals.method: We will extend a vision-language transformer (e.g., a CLIP-style architecture) using a last-layer Laplace approximation for efficient epistemic uncertainty quantification and a heteroscedastic output head for aleatoric uncertainty. The model will be pretrained on large-scale medical image-text datasets (e.g., MIMIC-CXR with radiology reports) using a modified contrastive loss that incorporates uncertainty penalties. We will then fine-tune and evaluate on chest CT tasks from Yeung's prior work, benchmarking uncertainty calibration against ensemble methods and Monte Carlo Dropout.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a scalable framework for uncertainty-aware multimodal medical pretraining, a benchmark for uncertainty quantification in medical vision-language tasks, and empirical evidence on how pretraining affects uncertainty calibration. It will provide tools to diagnose model reliability directly from its representations.
proposals.novelty: While uncertainty methods exist, their integration into the multimodal pretraining paradigm for medical AI is unexplored. The novelty lies in quantifying uncertainty not just in final predictions but within the joint image-text embedding space, linking it to model interpretability and safety.

proposals.title: Generating Coherent Radiology Reports from Chest CT Scans via Vision-Language Pretraining and Task-Specific Tuning
proposals.motivation: Automated generation of radiology reports from medical images can alleviate clinician workload and reduce reporting delays, directly impacting patient care. Current methods often produce templated or incoherent text, failing to capture the nuanced, free-form narrative style of expert radiologists. This project addresses the core challenge of generating clinically accurate and linguistically fluent reports directly from 3D volumetric data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model, first pretrained on a large-scale, diverse corpus of paired medical images and text to learn aligned vision-language representations, and then fine-tuned on a curated radiology report dataset with a carefully designed task formulation, can generate comprehensive and clinically useful radiology reports from chest CT scans.
proposals.method: We will build upon the success of Video Pretraining (VP) for 3D CT tasks by adapting a video-capable transformer (e.g., a TimeSformer or Video Swin Transformer backbone) as our visual encoder. This encoder will be integrated with a large language model (LLM) decoder via a cross-attention mechanism, following architectures like Flamingo or BLIP-2. The model will be first pretrained on a broad dataset of medical image-text pairs (e.g., MIMIC-CXR, ROCO) to learn general medical vision-language alignment. Subsequently, it will be fine-tuned on a curated dataset of chest CT volumes and their corresponding full-text reports, using a next-token prediction loss conditioned on the encoded CT scan.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, end-to-end model for generating full narrative radiology reports from 3D CT scans, moving beyond classification or captioning. It will provide a rigorous benchmark for report generation on chest CT and offer insights into the transferability of video-based 3D pretraining to structured language generation tasks in medicine.
proposals.novelty: While image captioning exists, generating long-form, structured reports from 3D medical volumes is largely unexplored. The novelty lies in the specific adaptation of 3D video architectures for dense language generation and the two-stage training strategy that separates general medical concept alignment from domain-specific report style learning.

proposals.title: Jointly Learned Visual Representations for Adaptive and Efficient Visual Navigation in Medical Imaging
proposals.motivation: In visual navigation tasks, such as those for analyzing medical scans, agents typically use a fixed, pre-trained CNN (e.g., ImageNet) to extract features from image glimpses. This creates a fundamental mismatch: the features are optimized for generic object recognition, not for the specific decision-making policy of the agent. This can lead to suboptimal performance, inefficiency, and poor generalization in specialized domains like medical imaging where task-relevant visual patterns are unique.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that jointly learning the feature extraction CNN with the agent's reinforcement learning policy will yield visual representations that are more efficient, task-aligned, and ultimately lead to superior navigation performance on domain-specific tasks like lesion localization in 3D medical volumes.
proposals.method: We will develop an end-to-end reinforcement learning agent where the initial convolutional layers are not frozen. The agent, based on an Asynchronous Advantage Actor-Critic (A3C) or Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) framework, will process raw pixel glimpses. The gradients from the policy and value heads will flow back through the CNN encoder, allowing it to learn features directly relevant to maximizing the navigation reward. We will benchmark this against fixed-feature baselines on tasks built using public chest CT datasets (e.g., NIH DeepLesion) for target finding.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate the performance benefits of task-adaptive visual feature learning for embodied AI in medicine. It will provide a framework for training more sample-efficient and accurate visual navigation agents, moving beyond the reliance on generic, pre-trained vision models. The resulting method could generalize to other data-scarce, visually complex domains.
proposals.novelty: While joint training is common in end-to-end robotics, applying this principle to challenge the standard paradigm of fixed, pre-trained glimpse encoders in visual navigation for medical image analysis is novel. It directly connects Yeung's expertise in medical vision, video pretraining, and diagnostic models with core reinforcement learning.

proposals.title: Cross-Modal Federated Personalization via Unsupervised Feature Alignment and Model Ensembling
proposals.motivation: Personalized AI for healthcare and other sensitive domains is limited by data silos and strict privacy regulations like HIPAA. Clients often possess data in distinct modalities (e.g., CT scans, clinical notes), making direct data pooling or federated learning across modalities infeasible due to distribution shifts and privacy risks. A method is needed to leverage knowledge from diverse, private datasets without sharing raw data or compromising model performance on a new client's unique modality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that unsupervised domain adaptation can align disparate client data distributions into a shared feature space, and that a server can personalize a model for a new client by ensembling frozen, pre-trained client models using only their aligned feature representations as a similarity guide, without parameter updates.
proposals.method: Each client uses an unsupervised domain adaptation method, such as a variant of CycleGAN or adversarial feature alignment, to project its local data into a predefined, modality-agnostic feature space. Clients send only their final, frozen model and a statistical summary of their adapted feature distribution to a server. For a new client, the server performs a similarity analysis (e.g., Maximum Mean Discrepancy) between the new client's adapted features and the stored feature summaries. It then constructs a personalized model via a weighted ensemble or distillation from the k-most-similar client models, avoiding gradient sharing.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for privacy-preserving, cross-modal personalization that operates under a strict 'data-free, gradient-free' constraint. It will provide a benchmark and methodology for evaluating personalization performance across heterogeneous modalities in medical imaging and vision-language tasks, advancing the field of federated learning beyond homogeneous data assumptions.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard federated learning which shares gradients or model averaging (FedAvg), this approach shares only final models and aligned feature statistics, decoupling personalization from iterative training. It uniquely combines unsupervised domain adaptation as a client-side pre-processing step with model ensembling based on feature-space similarity for cross-modal transfer.

proposals.title: Topological Characterization of Loss Landscapes: A Persistent Homology Framework for Analyzing and Improving Deep Learning Optimization
proposals.motivation: Understanding the geometry of high-dimensional, non-convex loss landscapes remains a fundamental challenge in deep learning theory, with direct implications for optimization and generalization. Current empirical analyses, like loss surface visualizations via random projections, are limited in capturing the complex topological features that govern training dynamics. A principled, quantitative framework is needed to move beyond intuition and connect landscape structure to model performance.
proposals.hypothesis: Topological features of the loss landscape, quantified via persistent homology, correlate strongly with optimization efficiency (e.g., convergence rate, plateau escape) and final model generalization. Furthermore, interventions guided by topological signatures during training can improve optimization outcomes.
proposals.method: We will develop scalable methods to sample high-dimensional loss surfaces during the training of vision models (e.g., ResNets, Vision Transformers) on medical imaging datasets (e.g., CheXpert). Using these samples, we will compute persistence diagrams to summarize topological features like the birth and death of connected components and cycles. We will correlate these topological summaries with optimization metrics (gradient norms, loss curves) and final test performance. Building on this, we will explore topology-aware training interventions, such as adjusting learning rates or injecting noise when persistent homology indicates a problematic landscape structure (e.g., a proliferation of persistent local minima).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, empirically grounded topological framework for analyzing deep learning optimization, yielding new metrics to diagnose training difficulties. It will also demonstrate the potential of topology-aware training strategies, offering a new direction for optimizer design that moves beyond local gradient information.
proposals.novelty: While TDA has been applied to neural network architecture analysis and data topology, its direct application to characterize the *optimization loss landscape* of modern, large-scale deep networks is novel. The analogy to protein folding energy landscapes provides a rigorous, interdisciplinary foundation distinct from prior convex or linear mode connectivity analyses.

proposals.title: Optimization-Induced Fairness Disparities: Diagnosing and Mitigating Geometric Bias in Non-Convex Learning
proposals.motivation: Deep learning models, especially in vision and medical domains, often exhibit fairness disparities across protected groups. Current research primarily treats these as data or metric problems, ignoring the role of the non-convex optimization process itself. Given the known phenomena of modality gaps and sensitivity to initialization in high-dimensional losses, it is critical to understand if standard training dynamics inherently steer models toward biased representations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the geometry of non-convex loss landscapes—specifically, the prevalence of saddle points and the selection of flat vs. sharp minima—systematically influences the separation of learned representations for different protected groups, thereby creating or amplifying fairness disparities that are not solely attributable to dataset bias.
proposals.method: We will develop a theoretical framework linking optimization trajectories on non-convex losses to the geometry of final representations. Empirically, we will analyze large-scale pretrained vision-language models (e.g., CLIP-style) and medical imaging models (e.g., chest CT networks) using controlled datasets with protected attributes. We will track optimization paths, measure emerging modality/representation gaps, and correlate them with fairness metrics. Novel interventions, such as loss landscape-aware regularization or saddle-point escape mechanisms tailored for group fairness, will be designed and tested.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, optimization-centric explanation for the emergence of algorithmic bias, moving beyond post-hoc analysis. It will yield practical, training-time interventions to promote fairness by directly shaping the learning dynamics, contributing new tools for equitable model development in medical and vision AI.
proposals.novelty: While fairness and optimization are well-studied separately, this directly connects non-convex optimization dynamics to the geometric origin of disparities, proposing to diagnose and rectify bias through the lens of loss landscape geometry rather than just data distributions or output metrics.

proposals.title: Diagnosing and Rectifying Non-Convex Optimization in Medical Vision Models via Multi-Task and Multi-Modal Pretraining
proposals.motivation: Modern deep learning for medical vision relies on non-convex models where optimization dynamics are poorly understood, leading to inconsistent performance and difficulty in diagnosing failures. Pre-2026, scaling laws and large-scale pretraining have driven progress, but their theoretical underpinnings and empirical effects in complex, non-convex regimes—especially for multi-modal (image+language) and multi-task (e.g., 2D/3D) medical models—remain unclear. This gap hinders the reliable development of robust clinical AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the optimization landscape of scaled, multi-modal pretrained models for medical vision possesses tractable structure (e.g., connectivity of minima, loss basin geometry) that can be diagnosed using language-guided probes and rectified via targeted multi-task fine-tuning, leading to more predictable and robust performance.
proposals.method: We will extend the 'Diagnosing and Rectifying' framework to non-convex settings by analyzing vision-language models (e.g., adaptations of CLIP) and 3D video-pretrained models (inspired by Video Pretraining work) on medical datasets (e.g., chest CT). We will empirically map optimization landscapes using techniques like loss basin visualization and mode connectivity analysis. Language will be used to generate diagnostic probes for model failures, and rectification will be achieved via constrained fine-tuning on a mixture of tasks (2D classification, 3D segmentation) to steer optimization towards more robust minima.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel empirical and theoretical analysis of optimization in modern, scaled non-convex networks for medical vision. It will yield practical algorithms for diagnosing instability and improving model robustness via multi-modal guidance, directly impacting the reliability of AI-assisted diagnosis. The resulting insights will bridge scaling practices with non-convex optimization theory.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes convex approximations or studies pretraining empirically, this proposal uniquely combines landscape analysis of scaled non-convex models with a language-driven diagnostic and rectification loop, specifically tailored to the multi-modal and multi-task paradigms central to recent advances in medical AI.

proposals.title: Critic-Guided Robust Distillation for Federated Learning: Enhancing Generalization and Adversarial Robustness in Medical Vision
proposals.motivation: Federated learning (FL) for medical imaging faces a critical tension: personalized client models adapt to local data distributions but can overfit to local biases or adversarial vulnerabilities. Directly aggregating these models (e.g., via FedAvg) risks creating a global model that inherits and amplifies these flaws, compromising its safety and generalizability. A robust mechanism is needed to distill a trustworthy global model from a federation of potentially flawed or heterogeneous local experts.
proposals.hypothesis: A learned critic network, trained on a small, curated validation set to evaluate the reliability of client models on representative anchor data, can guide a selective knowledge distillation process to produce a global model with superior generalization and adversarial robustness compared to standard federated aggregation.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage framework. First, after federated personalization, a central critic network is trained via meta-learning on a small validation set of diverse, challenging 'anchor' images (including adversarial examples). The critic learns to output a reliability score for each client's predictions. Second, these scores weight the contributions of client logits during a centralized distillation phase to train the global 'student' model. We will adapt vision-language models (inspired by 'Diagnosing and Rectifying Vision Models using Language') to generate semantically rich anchor points and leverage large-scale medical video pretraining strategies ('Video Pretraining Advances 3D Deep Learning') for robust feature initialization.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel FL aggregation framework that explicitly prioritizes robust client behaviors, 2) a new benchmark for evaluating FL robustness in medical vision, combining generalization and adversarial metrics, and 3) empirical validation on chest CT and other medical imaging datasets, demonstrating improved performance on held-out and adversarially perturbed data.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior robust aggregation methods that use simple statistical measures, we introduce a trainable critic for nuanced behavioral evaluation. Furthermore, we reframe robust FL not as direct parameter averaging but as a critic-guided distillation process, a novel perspective for the field.

proposals.title: Learning the Optimal Modality Gap: A Dynamic Optimal Transport Framework for Task-Informed Multimodal Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Multimodal models, especially in vision-language and medical imaging, often rely on contrastive or feature-matching losses that force a rigid alignment of embeddings across modalities. Pre-2026 work shows this can discard useful modality-specific information and harm generalization. A principled, geometry-aware method is needed to learn *how much* and *where* to align modalities for a specific downstream task.
proposals.hypothesis: Using Optimal Transport (OT) as a dynamic, differentiable regularizer during multimodal pretraining will allow a model to discover a task-informed 'optimal gap'—a structured arrangement of modality embeddings—leading to more efficient and generalizable representations than those from naive forced alignment.
proposals.method: We will develop a training framework where a Sinkhorn-based OT distance between mini-batch distributions of image and text (or other modality) embeddings is computed online. This OT distance, which respects the geometry of the distributions, will be integrated as a dynamic regularization term alongside a primary task loss (e.g., contrastive or classification). We will implement this within a scalable vision-language architecture (e.g., a ViT/CNN encoder paired with a transformer text encoder) and test it on established medical (e.g., MIMIC-CXR) and general (e.g., Conceptual Captions) datasets, benchmarking against standard alignment methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel training paradigm that uses OT for active modality gap manipulation, not just measurement; 2) empirical evidence on medical and vision-language tasks that a learned, structured gap outperforms full alignment; and 3) a publicly released codebase for the dynamic OT regularizer.
proposals.novelty: While OT has been used *post-hoc* to analyze modality gaps, using it as a dynamic, trainable signal to *learn* an optimal gap configuration is a novel departure from static metric application or simple distance minimization like MSE or cosine loss.

proposals.title: Learning to Scale: Instance-Adaptive Temperature Fields for Robust Contrastive Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: In contrastive learning, the temperature parameter critically controls the hardness of negative sampling and the concentration of embeddings, but is typically a single global scalar. This is suboptimal for complex, multi-modal datasets common in medical imaging and video understanding, where the semantic density and local manifold structure vary significantly across the data distribution. A fixed temperature can lead to either over-concentrated clusters or excessively diffuse representations, limiting downstream task performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that learning a structured, instance-adaptive temperature field, regularized to maintain stability, will yield more robust and semantically meaningful representations by dynamically calibrating the contrastive loss based on local data structure, outperforming fixed or annealed global temperature schedules.
proposals.method: We will augment a standard contrastive encoder (e.g., ResNet or Vision Transformer) with a lightweight Temperature Prediction Network (TPN) that takes the anchor's projected embedding and statistics (e.g., variance, k-nearest neighbor distances) from its local batch neighborhood as input to predict a positive scalar temperature τ_i. The contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE) will use this instance-specific τ_i. A regularization loss, combining a Kullback-Leibler divergence from a prior log-normal distribution and a penalty on extreme gradients, will be applied to the TPN's outputs to ensure stable training and prevent collapse. We will validate on medical video (e.g., chest CT) and multi-modal (image-language) datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled framework for adaptive temperature scaling in contrastive learning, moving beyond heuristic tuning. It will deliver improved representation quality, measured by linear probe and fine-tuning performance on challenging vision tasks, and offer new insights into the relationship between local data manifold structure and optimal contrastive loss geometry.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored temperature annealing or learned global parameters, the idea of a spatially/topologically structured temperature field predicted from instance and neighborhood features is novel. The regularization strategy to tame this field distinguishes it from simple parameterization and addresses a key stability concern.

proposals.title: FedTemp: Client-Adaptive Temperature Scaling for Harmonized Federated Contrastive Learning in Medical Imaging
proposals.motivation: Cross-silo federated learning for medical imaging enables multi-institutional collaboration without sharing sensitive patient data. However, severe statistical heterogeneity (non-IID data) across hospitals degrades model performance, as a single global contrastive loss cannot account for varying local data densities and semantic structures. Existing methods for federated contrastive learning lack mechanisms to dynamically adapt the learning objective to each client's intrinsic data distribution, leading to suboptimal and biased global representations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that learning a personalized temperature parameter per client, which quantifies the local semantic density and hardness of negative pairs, and aggregating these signals at the server will calibrate the global contrastive loss, leading to more robust and harmonized representations across heterogeneous medical data silos.
proposals.method: We will extend a federated SimCLR or MoCo-v2 framework. Each client will optimize its local encoder alongside a learnable, scalar temperature parameter (τ_i) within its contrastive loss. Local τ_i values, acting as privacy-preserving distributional summaries, will be sent to the server and aggregated via a robust mean. The server will broadcast the aggregated global temperature to guide the next round of local training. We will validate on federated versions of medical image datasets (e.g., CheXpert, MIMIC-CXR) under realistic non-IID partitions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, lightweight method to directly address data heterogeneity in federated contrastive learning via personalized loss calibration, 2) empirical evidence that client-adaptive temperature improves convergence and final performance on downstream tasks like classification across diverse institutional data, and 3) a new perspective on using hyperparameters as federated signals for data distribution alignment.
proposals.novelty: While learnable temperature is explored in centralized contrastive learning, its personalization and federation for cross-silo medical imaging is novel. The approach innovates by making a core loss hyperparameter a client-specific, communicable entity to mitigate statistical heterogeneity, differing from prior work focused solely on model parameter aggregation or regularization.

proposals.title: Learning a Common Semantic Substrate for Robust Cross-Modal Medical Vision-Language Tasks
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models for medicine, such as those interpreting chest CTs or radiology reports, often fuse features from distinct encoders directly, leading to a 'modality gap' that hinders semantic alignment and robust reasoning. This gap is a critical bottleneck for tasks like cross-modal retrieval of medical images by text or generating accurate image descriptions, which require tight integration of visual and linguistic semantics. Drawing inspiration from cognitive neuroscience's 'common code' theory for multisensory integration, we posit that explicitly learning a shared intermediate representation can bridge this gap more effectively than existing late or early fusion techniques.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly constructing a task-optimized, common representational substrate—a learned intermediate semantic space—for projecting features from vision and language encoders will yield superior performance on cross-modal medical tasks compared to direct fusion across the original modality gap. This substrate will enforce a unified geometry where semantically similar concepts from different modalities are closely aligned.
proposals.method: We will design a dual-encoder architecture (e.g., a 3D CNN for CT volumes and a transformer for text) pretrained on large-scale datasets (MIMIC-CXR, RadGraph). Instead of fusing their outputs directly, we will project both into a learned 'Common Semantic Substrate' (CSS) via separate lightweight adapter networks. The CSS will be trained with a combination of contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE) for cross-modal alignment and a task-specific loss (e.g., for retrieval or VQA). We will benchmark against standard fusion baselines (e.g., concatenation, cross-attention) on curated medical vision-language tasks requiring fine-grained reasoning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, cognitively-inspired framework for cross-modal learning in medical AI, moving beyond treating the modality gap as a nuisance to actively engineering a bridge. It will deliver a scalable method for tighter vision-language integration, validated on clinically relevant tasks. The resulting models and the concept of an explicit substrate could generalize to other multimodal domains in Serena Yeung's research, such as video and sensor data fusion.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses shared latent spaces or alignment losses, the explicit design and optimization of an intermediate 'substrate' as a dedicated semantic bridge is novel. This approach is distinct from simply aligning embeddings, as it constructs a new representational space explicitly purposed for unified semantics before any fusion or decision-making, a concept underexplored in pre-2026 medical vision-language research.

proposals.title: Task-Adaptive Geometric Fusion: Learning Dynamic Modality Overlap for Medical Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models for medicine, such as those for chest CT analysis, enforce a single, static geometry for modality representations (e.g., via contrastive pretraining). This is suboptimal because different clinical tasks require different relationships between visual and textual data: dense retrieval of imaging findings benefits from tight alignment, while compositional reasoning for diagnosis may require preserving modality-specific, complementary information. A universal alignment metric fails to capture this task-dependent need.
proposals.hypothesis: The optimal geometric relationship between vision and language embeddings is a learnable, task-dependent property, and dynamically adjusting the overlap between modality representations based on a meta-objective will improve performance on diverse medical AI tasks compared to static fusion strategies.
proposals.method: We will extend a pretrained medical VLM (e.g., based on VideoMAE or a CT-pretrained encoder) with a lightweight, task-conditioned gating network. This network will take task embeddings and intermediate feature maps as input to produce parameters for a dynamic alignment loss, modulating the strength of contrastive (CLIP-style) versus modality-separation (e.g., orthogonal regularization) terms during fine-tuning. We will validate the approach on a curated multi-task benchmark combining chest CT tasks (nodule retrieval, report generation) and medical visual QA, using scaling studies on large-scale datasets like MIMIC-CXR and internal CT cohorts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new framework for task-aware multimodal fusion, moving beyond static alignment. It will yield a model capable of superior performance across heterogeneous medical tasks by optimizing representational geometry. We will also release a benchmark and analysis toolkit for diagnosing modality interaction in clinical VLMs.
proposals.novelty: While prior work diagnoses or rectifies model behavior post-hoc, our method proactively learns to shape the joint embedding space geometry as a function of the task, a departure from fixed fusion objectives like universal feature alignment or late fusion.

proposals.title: Task-Aware Modality Gap Tuning: A Systematic Framework for Optimizing Vision-Language Representations
proposals.motivation: Vision-language models (VLMs) like CLIP create a joint embedding space, but the optimal distance (modality gap) between image and text features is unknown. While a narrow gap is assumed beneficial for zero-shot classification, its impact on diverse downstream tasks—such as dense prediction (e.g., segmentation), retrieval, or visual question answering—remains unexplored. This gap is a critical hyperparameter for fine-tuning and adaptation, yet current practice uses a one-size-fits-all approach, potentially limiting performance.
proposals.hypothesis: The optimal modality gap for a pre-trained VLM is not universal but is task-dependent, and systematically tuning this gap during task-specific adaptation will yield significant performance improvements across a spectrum of vision-language tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a controlled tuning framework using established VLMs (e.g., CLIP, ALIGN) as a base. For a suite of downstream tasks (semantic segmentation on medical imaging, video-text retrieval, VQA), we will introduce a learnable, task-specific projection layer that can dynamically adjust the alignment between visual and textual feature streams during fine-tuning. We will measure the effective gap using metrics like average cosine distance and correlate it with task performance. The core experiment will involve ablating different constraint losses (e.g., contrastive, cosine similarity) to widen or narrow the gap and evaluate the resulting task-specific embeddings.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical map linking modality gap geometry to downstream task performance, moving beyond zero-shot classification. It will yield a practical tuning methodology for adapting VLMs to specialized domains, like medical vision, where data is scarce and representation structure is crucial. We will release a benchmark for evaluating gap-aware adaptation strategies.
proposals.novelty: Prior work diagnoses or minimizes the modality gap; we propose to treat it as a tunable, task-specific resource. This shifts the paradigm from seeking a single optimal alignment to learning a spectrum of alignments optimized for different objectives, which is novel given the prevailing focus on gap reduction in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Kriging the Modality Gap: A Geostatistical Framework for Optimal Transport-Based Multimodal Alignment
proposals.motivation: Multimodal models, especially vision-language models, rely on aligned embedding spaces, but a persistent 'modality gap' hinders zero-shot transfer and performance. Pre-2026, Optimal Transport (OT) has been used to measure this gap, but lacks a principled model for its structure. Geostatistics provides a mature framework for modeling spatial correlations and performing optimal interpolation, which is directly analogous to mapping between distributed point clouds in embedding space.
proposals.hypothesis: The structural relationship between multimodal embeddings (e.g., image and text) can be modeled via a transportogram, analogous to a geostatistical variogram, and this model enables optimal, kriging-like interpolation between modalities that outperforms naive distance minimization.
proposals.method: We will first define and compute a transportogram: for pairs of aligned image-text embedding sets, we calculate their OT distance (e.g., using Sinkhorn algorithm) and plot it against a measure of semantic alignment (e.g., label consistency) to model the inter-modal correlation structure. Second, we will develop a kriging-inspired algorithm. This algorithm will use the fitted transportogram model as a kernel to perform best linear unbiased prediction (BLUP), taking a new text embedding as an input 'location' and predicting the corresponding image embedding that minimizes the expected OT-based prediction error, effectively performing structured cross-modal mapping.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, model-based framework for quantifying the nuanced structure of the modality gap, moving beyond scalar distance measures. It will yield a practical algorithm for intelligent cross-modal interpolation, potentially improving zero-shot retrieval and generation in multimodal systems. The core contribution is the adaptation of a rigorous spatial statistics paradigm to a core machine learning problem.
proposals.novelty: While OT is established in ML, the explicit adaptation of the geostatistical kriging pipeline—variogram modeling to optimal interpolation—to model and bridge the modality gap is novel. This provides a structured, probabilistic alternative to common heuristics like cosine similarity or unregularized OT matching.

proposals.title: MetaGap: Learning Adaptive Geometric Priors for Modality Alignment in Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: Aligning data from different modalities (e.g., images and text, CT scans and reports) is fundamental for multimodal AI in medicine and vision. Pre-2026, methods often use fixed geometric penalties (e.g., contrastive loss, uniform margin) to control the 'modality gap,' but these rigid constraints can distort task-relevant semantic structures. A data-driven approach to geometry is needed to preserve downstream performance while ensuring effective alignment.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing fixed geometric penalties with a learnable, instance-adaptive regularizer, conditioned on the statistics of the joint embedding space, will yield a more optimal and uniform modality gap structure, improving both alignment quality and downstream task performance.
proposals.method: We propose a meta-network that takes as input batch statistics (e.g., inter/intra-modality distance distributions, variance ratios) from the current joint embedding space of a pretrained vision-language model. This lightweight network, trained via a bi-level optimization loop, outputs adaptive constraint parameters (e.g., a soft margin, a coefficient for a uniformity loss) for each training batch. These parameters gently regularize the primary contrastive or alignment loss during fine-tuning, dynamically shaping the geometry. We will validate on medical (e.g., chest CT-text) and general vision-language datasets, benchmarking against fixed-penalty baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, data-driven framework for modality gap control, moving beyond hand-designed geometric losses. It will deliver a practical method for improving multimodal alignment in medical imaging and vision-language tasks, likely leading to more robust representations for downstream applications like diagnosis or retrieval.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the shift from a static, predefined geometric penalty to a dynamic, learned regularizer that adapts to observed embedding statistics, which is a non-obvious extension of existing contrastive and metric learning techniques.

proposals.title: Task-Optimal Geometric Regularization for Cross-Modal Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models often enforce a rigid, task-agnostic alignment between modalities (e.g., via contrastive loss), which may not be optimal for specific downstream tasks like medical VQA or cross-modal retrieval. A one-size-fits-all alignment can discard task-critical information or create suboptimal latent geometries for fusion. This work addresses the need for a principled, task-aware method to shape the joint embedding space for superior multimodal reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dynamically regularizing the latent geometry of multimodal embeddings based on direct feedback from a target task's performance will yield more effective representations than static, task-agnostic alignment objectives, leading to improved accuracy and data efficiency.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework building on large-scale pretrained encoders (e.g., CLIP). First, we will define a suite of differentiable geometric penalties (e.g., intra/inter-modality distance ratios, local manifold curvature metrics) applied to the latent representations. Second, we will introduce a meta-learning or hypernetwork controller that modulates the strength of each geometric penalty based on the gradient of the validation loss of the target fusion task (e.g., a VQA head). This creates a closed-loop system where task performance directly informs the geometric regularization strategy during fine-tuning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel learning paradigm for task-aware multimodal representation learning, moving beyond uniform alignment. It will provide a new, interpretable toolkit for geometric regularization in latent spaces and demonstrate performance gains on challenging benchmarks in medical imaging (e.g., chest CT-VQA) and general-domain cross-modal retrieval.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the closed-loop, performance-driven adaptation of geometric constraints, rather than applying fixed penalties. This shifts the objective from minimizing a generic 'modality gap' to learning a task-optimal geometry, a non-obvious direction given the dominance of static contrastive or reconstruction-based alignment in pre-2026 work.

proposals.title: Edge-Personalized Vision-Language Models via Latent Reprojection: A Zero-Update Approach for Tiny Architectures
proposals.motivation: Deploying personalized multi-modal AI on edge devices (e.g., for assistive health monitoring) is constrained by the computational cost of fine-tuning and privacy risks of sharing raw data. While large models can be adapted, tiny models (<10M params) are essential for on-device use but suffer from a pronounced modality gap, making personalization via conventional weight updates inefficient and insecure.
proposals.hypothesis: The alignment gap between vision and language embeddings in frozen, tiny multi-modal models can be predictably manipulated for personalization by learning a minimal, data-dependent linear or lightweight non-linear transformation that reprojects one modality's latent space, using only local private data and without performing gradient-based fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We will first pretrain or adapt a tiny dual-encoder model (e.g., a minimized CLIP variant) on a public dataset. For personalization, given a user's private image-text pairs, we will freeze all model weights and learn a small reprojection network (e.g., a single linear layer or tiny MLP) that maps image embeddings to better align with the text embedding space (or vice-versa). This will be trained via a contrastive loss on the private data. We will experiment with methods from 'Diagnosing and Rectifying Vision Models using Language' to analyze the gap and design the transformation, evaluating on personalized medical image captioning and retrieval tasks derived from chest CT and general vision datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, privacy-preserving paradigm for personalizing tiny multi-modal models that eliminates the need for weight updates, reducing communication and computation overhead. It will provide new insights into the geometry and manipulability of the modality gap in highly constrained models, bridging fundamental representation learning with practical edge-AI deployment.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior personalization via fine-tuning or prompt-tuning, this approach proposes manipulating the frozen model's latent geometry itself with a learned reprojection, a concept unexplored for tiny models. It unites the analysis of modality gaps with a secure, update-free personalization mechanism, moving beyond scaling and pretraining to efficient post-deployment adaptation.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for the Modality Gap: An Empirical Study of Vision-Language Alignment with Web-Scale Datasets
proposals.motivation: The 'modality gap'—a measurable geometric separation between image and text embeddings in multimodal models—is a known phenomenon that can hinder cross-modal retrieval and zero-shot generalization. Prior studies (e.g., Radford et al., 2021) have characterized it in models trained on hundreds of millions of samples, but it is unknown how this gap evolves with orders-of-magnitude more data, a key scaling question for next-generation models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the modality gap does not vanish with increased data scale but instead undergoes a predictable phase transition: initially narrowing with improved alignment before saturating or restructuring into a more complex, task-relevant separation geometry.
proposals.method: We will scale up contrastive vision-language pretraining (using CLIP and ALIGN-style architectures) on curated subsets of the LAION-5B dataset and proprietary datasets exceeding 10B image-text pairs. We will track the evolution of the gap using metrics like mean embedding distance, canonical correlation analysis, and performance on diagnostic probes from Yeung's prior work. Controlled experiments will vary dataset size, curation strategy, and model capacity to isolate scaling effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical scaling laws for the modality gap, offering practical guidance for dataset curation and model design in massive multimodal training. It will also deliver new diagnostic tools to understand when and why scaling fails to improve alignment, advancing the science of multimodal representation learning.
proposals.novelty: While scaling is well-studied for unimodal performance, its systematic effect on the *geometric relationship* between modalities is unexplored. This proposal uniquely combines Yeung's expertise in vision-language diagnostics with a large-scale, controlled scaling study beyond published dataset sizes.

proposals.title: Vision-Language Guided Contrastive Clustering for Open-World Object Discovery in Medical Imaging
proposals.motivation: In real-world medical vision applications, deployed models inevitably encounter unknown anatomical findings, pathologies, or instrument types not present in their training sets. Current systems either misclassify these or treat them as unstructured noise, hindering safe incremental learning and continuous dataset curation. This problem is acute in domains like chest CT or surgical video analysis, where new object categories must be systematically discovered and labeled for model updating without full human supervision.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a joint visual feature clustering and language-guided refinement framework, leveraging large-scale pretrained vision-language models (VLMs), can accurately group and propose semantically meaningful labels for unknown objects detected in medical images, forming a reliable basis for incremental learning.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage method. First, we will extract features for detected unknown object regions using a VLM (e.g., CLIP) image encoder fine-tuned on medical data. We will then apply a scalable, density-based clustering algorithm (like Hierarchical Density-Based Spatial Clustering, HDBSCAN) on these features to form initial visual groups. Second, we will use the VLM's text encoder to generate and score candidate label proposals for each cluster via prompt ensembling (e.g., "a photo of a [CLASS] in a chest CT"), drawing from a broad biomedical vocabulary. The most confident label-cluster pairs will be output for human verification and subsequent model fine-tuning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel pipeline for open-world object discovery in medical vision that moves beyond pure visual similarity to semantically informed clustering, and 2) a new benchmark and dataset for evaluating unknown object categorization in incremental learning scenarios, derived from existing chest CT and surgical video datasets.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive visual clustering exists, the integration of a medical-pretrained VLM for simultaneous cluster formation and semantic label proposal is novel. This directly addresses the semantic gap between visual clusters and actionable class names, a key bottleneck for incremental learning in specialized domains.

proposals.title: Diffusion-Based Probabilistic Feature Modeling for Open-World Recognition in Medical Vision
proposals.motivation: In medical image analysis, deep learning models often fail when encountering novel anatomical variations or pathologies not seen during training, leading to overconfident and erroneous predictions. This limitation is critical for safe clinical deployment. While current anomaly detection methods use simplistic density estimators (e.g., Gaussian models) on pretrained features, they struggle with the complex, multi-modal distributions of real medical data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling the feature space of known medical image classes with a sophisticated generative model, specifically a diffusion model, will yield a more accurate and calibrated probability distribution, enabling reliable identification of out-of-distribution (unknown) samples and improving model robustness.
proposals.method: We will build on large-scale, pretrained video models (e.g., from chest CT pretraining) to extract spatio-temporal features. Instead of standard Gaussian Mixture Models, we will train a conditional denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM) to learn the complex distribution of features for each known class. At inference, the likelihood of a new sample will be estimated by the diffusion model's evidence lower bound (ELBO). We will integrate this with a language-guided rectification module to diagnose and categorize failure modes, creating a unified framework for open-world evaluation on curated medical vision datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, diffusion-based framework for probabilistic open-world recognition in medical imaging. It will produce a new benchmark dataset for evaluating unknown object detection in chest CT, and provide insights into how generative modeling scale improves uncertainty quantification compared to prior parametric methods.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying state-of-the-art diffusion models—typically used for image synthesis—to the distinct problem of modeling deep feature distributions for open-set medical diagnosis, and synergistically combining this with language-based model introspection techniques.

proposals.title: Contrastive Proposal Embedding for Robust Object Localization in Medical and Satellite Imagery
proposals.motivation: In medical imaging (e.g., chest CT) and satellite image analysis, accurate object localization (e.g., lesions, vessels) is critical but challenged by complex backgrounds and limited annotations. Current region proposal networks (RPNs) often rely on supervised losses that may not learn optimally discriminative features for separating subtle objects from background clutter. A more structured feature space could improve detection robustness, especially in data-scarce domains where large-scale pretraining is beneficial.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying contrastive learning to region proposal embeddings will enforce a feature space where object proposals are more separable from background proposals, leading to more accurate and robust object localization, particularly in challenging visual domains like medical and satellite imagery.
proposals.method: We will extend a standard two-stage detector (e.g., Faster R-CNN) by adding a contrastive learning head on top of the RPN's region-of-interest (RoI) features. Positive pairs will be augmentations of the same object proposal, while negative pairs will be object vs. background proposals. We will employ a contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE) alongside the standard RPN classification and regression losses. The model will be pretrained on large-scale, diverse image datasets (leveraging scaling and pretraining themes) and fine-tuned on target medical (e.g., chest CT) and satellite (e.g., vessel detection) tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for improving region proposal quality through self-supervised feature structuring. It will deliver new state-of-the-art benchmarks for object detection in challenging visual domains where Yeung's group has expertise. The resulting 'contrastive proposal embedding' method will be a generalizable contribution to the vision and medical imaging communities.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning is established for image-level representation, its direct application to structure the intermediate region proposal feature space for object-background separation is novel. This bridges the gap between self-supervised pretraining and the specific sub-task of proposal generation within object detection pipelines.

proposals.title: PROB-FS: Scaling Down Prompt-Based Open-World Detection for Few-Shot Medical Imaging
proposals.motivation: Open-world object detection (OWOD) is critical for medical imaging, where novel pathologies or anatomical variants frequently appear. However, current state-of-the-art methods like PROB require extensive labeled data for known classes, which is a major bottleneck in clinical settings where expert annotations are scarce and expensive. Adapting PROB to work with only a few labeled examples per known class from the start would dramatically increase its practical utility in real-world diagnostic pipelines.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a language-guided, region-proposal-based pretraining strategy with a carefully designed few-shot fine-tuning stage, the PROB framework can be made robust to extreme label scarcity while retaining its strong open-world detection capabilities.
proposals.method: We will adapt the PROB (Prompt-Based) OWOD architecture, which uses a vision-language model (e.g., CLIP) to align region proposals with text prompts. The core adaptation involves a two-phase training regime: 1) A 'scale-down' pretraining phase using large-scale, unlabeled chest CT video data (inspired by Yeung's video pretraining work) to learn general visual representations and region proposal mechanisms. 2) A novel few-shot fine-tuning stage that uses a prototype-based contrastive loss on the limited labeled examples per known class, regularized by the language-model prompts to prevent catastrophic forgetting of the open-world capability. We will specifically test on chest CT datasets with very few annotated lesions per class.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new benchmark for few-shot open-world detection in medical vision, demonstrating that OWOD models can be data-efficient. It will provide a practical method for deploying adaptable diagnostic AI in low-resource clinical environments. The findings will also advance the theoretical understanding of how language-guided models generalize from few examples.
proposals.novelty: While PROB introduced language for OWOD, and few-shot learning is well-studied, their combination for medical detection—starting from extreme label scarcity—is unexplored. The novelty lies in the 'scale-down' pretraining strategy and the prototype-language alignment fine-tuning designed to preserve open-world readiness.

proposals.title: Temporal Consistency for Open-World Anomaly Detection in Medical Video
proposals.motivation: Open-world anomaly detection in medical imaging, such as identifying rare pathologies in endoscopic or surgical video, is a critical but unsolved challenge.
