Current vision models, often trained on static images, fail to leverage the rich temporal context inherent in video, which could provide crucial cues for detecting subtle, unknown anomalies through motion and appearance consistency over time. This limits their real-world clinical utility where novel findings must be flagged.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a video-pretrained foundation model, fine-tuned with a temporal consistency objective, will significantly outperform image-based models in detecting and localizing unknown anomalies in medical video sequences by learning robust spatiotemporal representations of normal anatomy.
proposals.method: We will build upon VideoMAE or similar masked autoencoder architectures for video pretraining, using large-scale natural video datasets. The model will be adapted to the medical domain via task-specific fine-tuning on curated public datasets of normal surgical or endoscopic video (e.g., Cholec80). The core technical innovation is a novel temporal consistency loss that enforces stable feature representations for normal tissue across frames, making deviations (anomalies) more salient. Detection will be performed by thresholding a reconstruction or feature discrepancy score aggregated over short temporal windows.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel framework for open-world anomaly detection in medical video that explicitly leverages temporal learning, 2) a new benchmark and protocol for evaluating such models on clinical video sequences, and 3) insights into how video pretraining and temporal objectives transfer to challenging, data-scarce medical domains.
proposals.novelty: While video pretraining and anomaly detection exist separately, their combination for open-world medical diagnosis is novel, as is the explicit use of a temporal consistency objective to define normality, moving beyond simple frame-by-frame reconstruction error.

proposals.title: The Data Pipeline Benchmark: Quantifying the Impact of Preprocessing and Augmentation on Medical Vision Model Performance
proposals.motivation: The machine learning community, especially in medical vision, heavily benchmarks static datasets (e.g., MIMIC-CXR, CheXpert) but treats the upstream data preparation pipeline as a fixed, unreported hyperparameter. This obscures the true drivers of model performance and hinders reproducibility. As scaling and pretraining become dominant, understanding the sensitivity of models to data curation choices—from raw data ingestion to final augmentation—is critical for robust and generalizable AI in healthcare.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that variations in the data preparation pipeline (e.g., curation heuristics, preprocessing steps, augmentation strategies) have a performance impact on par with or exceeding that of model architecture changes for medical vision tasks, and that this impact can be systematically quantified and modeled.
proposals.method: We will design a benchmark that treats the pipeline as a configurable module with discrete stages (source data filtering, label reconciliation, image normalization, pathology-specific augmentations). Using a fixed model architecture (e.g., a Vision Transformer or ResNet pretrained on chest CT/cXR), we will perform a controlled ablation across pipeline configurations on established datasets like CheXpert. We will employ methods from causal analysis and hyperparameter response modeling to isolate the contribution of each pipeline stage to final metrics like AUC and calibration error.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic framework for auditing and benchmarking data pipelines in medical vision, moving beyond dataset-centric evaluation. It will yield actionable insights into which pipeline stages most influence model robustness and generalization, enabling more reproducible and efficient model development. We will release a standardized benchmarking toolkit to catalyze more rigorous data-centric research.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on dataset quality or novel augmentation techniques, this proposal inverts the focus to benchmark the entire, configurable sequence of data transformations as a first-class object of study. It applies a rigorous, ablation-based methodology—common in architecture design—to the under-explored space of data preparation, specifically within the high-stakes domain of medical imaging.

proposals.title: MedALBench: A Multi-Modal Benchmark for Active Learning in Medical Vision
proposals.motivation: Data acquisition is a critical bottleneck in medical AI, where labeling is expensive and expert-dependent. While active learning (AL) promises to reduce labeling costs, its evaluation is fragmented across disparate datasets and tasks, making it difficult to compare strategies or understand their failure modes. A standardized, multi-modal benchmark is needed to systematically assess AL algorithms, particularly for vision-language models in medicine, where pre-2026 work shows promise but lacks rigorous evaluation frameworks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a comprehensive benchmark will reveal that the performance of active learning strategies is highly sensitive to dataset modality (e.g., 2D images vs. 3D CT vs. image-text pairs) and task complexity, and that no single strategy is optimal across all medical vision domains.
proposals.method: We will construct MedALBench by curating and standardizing a suite of pre-2026 medical vision datasets (e.g., Chest X-ray, dermatology images, chest CT volumes) with established labels. We will implement a core set of AL algorithms (e.g., uncertainty sampling, core-set, BALD) and acquisition functions, integrating them with standard vision backbones (ResNet, ViT) and vision-language models (CLIP-style). The benchmark will define evaluation protocols measuring accuracy vs. labeling cost, robustness to initial seed data, and computational efficiency, with a focus on multi-modal (image+text) query strategies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first standardized, multi-modal benchmark for evaluating data acquisition in medical vision, enabling direct comparison of AL algorithms. It will yield actionable insights into which strategies work best for specific data types and clinical tasks, guiding more efficient dataset creation for medical AI.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing general computer vision AL benchmarks, MedALBench focuses on the multi-modal and high-stakes medical domain, systematically evaluating the interaction between modern vision-language pretraining and active data acquisition, an underexplored area pre-2026.

proposals.title: Architecture-in-the-Loop: Benchmarking Model Families on Fixed, Imperfect Medical Imaging Datasets
proposals.motivation: Current AI research heavily benchmarks datasets on fixed model architectures, but real-world medical datasets are often fixed, imperfect, and expensive to re-collect. The inverse problem—systematically evaluating how different model architectures adapt to and exploit a given dataset's characteristics—is underexplored. This is critical for guiding architecture selection in domains like medical vision, where data constraints are permanent and performance is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a fixed, imperfect dataset (e.g., with label noise, sparsity, or domain shifts), the ranking of model architectures by performance will differ significantly from rankings on canonical, clean benchmarks, and that certain architectural inductive biases will be disproportionately effective at mitigating specific dataset flaws.
proposals.method: We will select a fixed, publicly available medical imaging dataset with known imperfections (e.g., CheXpert with label uncertainty). We will benchmark a diverse set of architectures (CNNs like ResNet, Vision Transformers, hybrid models, and models pretrained on video or language) under a controlled, scaling-aware protocol, varying only the architecture and its pretraining strategy. We will analyze performance not just by aggregate metrics but through detailed failure mode analysis, correlating architectural properties (e.g., spatial prior, attention range, pretraining modality) with robustness to specific dataset imperfections.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel benchmarking framework and empirical rankings to guide architecture selection for constrained data settings. It will yield actionable insights into which inductive biases best compensate for common data flaws, moving beyond the paradigm of simply collecting more data. The resulting analysis will be a resource for efficient model development in medical AI and other data-constrained fields.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard benchmarking paradigm to treat the dataset as the fixed substrate, a perspective aligned with real-world deployment constraints. It systematically connects architectural inductive biases to the mitigation of specific dataset imperfections, going beyond reporting aggregate scores.

proposals.title: ICU-Agnostic Adaptation: Few-Shot Visual Recognition for Clinical Monitoring via Meta-Learning from Robotic Vision
proposals.motivation: Deep learning models for clinical vision tasks, such as patient activity or device recognition from ICU video, fail to generalize across hospitals due to variations in equipment, layout, and camera viewpoints. Collecting and annotating large new datasets for each ICU is prohibitively expensive and slow, hindering the scalable deployment of assistive AI. This creates a critical need for models that can adapt to new clinical environments with minimal supervision.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that few-shot meta-learning algorithms, which have proven effective for rapid adaptation in robotic vision to new objects and environments, can be successfully repurposed to enable visual recognition models to adapt to new ICU settings using only a handful of annotated examples.
proposals.method: We will build upon Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) and its variants, pre-training a convolutional or vision transformer backbone on a large, diverse source dataset of ICU video (e.g., derived from public sources or multi-institutional collaborations). The meta-learning objective will simulate few-shot adaptation tasks, where the model learns initial parameters that are highly sensitive to new data. For a target ICU, adaptation will require only a small support set (e.g., 5-10 examples per class) of annotated frames or short clips. We will benchmark against standard fine-tuning and other transfer learning baselines on held-out target ICU data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical framework for data-efficient adaptation of clinical vision models, significantly lowering the annotation barrier for deployment in new hospitals. It will provide empirical evidence on the transferability of robotic few-shot learning techniques to the medical domain, and release a new benchmark protocol for evaluating ICU-agnostic visual recognition.
proposals.novelty: While few-shot learning is established in robotics and natural image domains, its systematic application to clinical video adaptation is novel. The key innovation is framing the inter-ICU domain shift problem as a meta-learning task, directly leveraging techniques from robotic vision where such adaptation to new environments is a core challenge.

proposals.title: CLIPath: Aligning Histopathology Images with Clinical Notes for Improved Prognostic Modeling
proposals.motivation: In computational pathology, models are typically trained on image data alone, ignoring the rich prognostic and diagnostic context found in associated clinical notes and pathology reports. This limits their ability to predict patient outcomes, which inherently depend on a fusion of morphological and clinical findings. Pre-2026, vision-language models like CLIP have shown remarkable success in aligning images and text in open domains, but their application to this dense, specialized medical multimodal problem remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a CLIP-like contrastive pretraining objective, applied to large-scale datasets of histopathology whole-slide image tiles and their corresponding clinical text snippets, will learn a joint embedding space that captures semantically meaningful clinical-pathological correlations, leading to superior performance on downstream prognostic tasks compared to image-only or late-fusion baselines.
proposals.method: We will adapt the CLIP architecture, using a Vision Transformer (ViT) pretrained on natural images as the image encoder and a clinical BERT model (e.g., Bio_ClinicalBERT) as the text encoder. The model will be trained via contrastive learning on a large-scale dataset of gigapixel whole-slide image (WSI) tiles paired with text from synoptic pathology reports and clinical notes. To handle the multiple-instance learning nature of WSIs, we will employ a cross-attention pooling mechanism to aggregate tile-level features into a slide-level representation before alignment with text. Downstream fine-tuning will be evaluated on tasks like survival prediction and recurrence risk stratification.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) A novel, publicly released vision-language model specifically pretrained for histopathology-clinical text alignment. 2) A methodological framework for multimodal fusion in pathology that moves beyond simple concatenation. 3) Empirical evidence demonstrating that joint representation learning from images and text improves prognostic accuracy over unimodal approaches, advancing towards more holistic computational pathology models.
proposals.novelty: While CLIP is established, its application to the precise alignment of histopathological morphology with dense, structured clinical language is novel. The key innovation is the tailored architecture and training strategy for gigapixel images and medical text, addressing the unique challenges of scale and clinical semantics not present in natural image-text pairs.

proposals.title: Federated Video Pretraining for Generalizable and Privacy-Preserving Medical Vision Models
proposals.motivation: Developing robust medical AI models requires large, diverse datasets, which are often siloed across hospitals due to patient privacy regulations like HIPAA. Centralizing sensitive medical video data, such as surgical recordings or ultrasound streams, is ethically and legally problematic. Federated learning (FL) offers a solution but has been underexplored for large-scale video pretraining, a method shown by Yeung et al. (2025) to advance performance on 3D medical tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a federated learning framework, built upon video pretraining objectives, can train a generalizable medical vision model that matches or exceeds the performance of centrally trained models while preserving data privacy across institutions.
proposals.method: We will adapt the FedAvg algorithm to coordinate training across multiple hospital clients, each holding proprietary video datasets (e.g., endoscopic videos). Each client will perform local pretraining using a masked autoencoder (MAE) objective on its video data. A central server will aggregate model weights periodically. We will then fine-tune the federated foundation model on specific downstream tasks (e.g., polyp detection in colonoscopy) using a separate, small annotated dataset, benchmarking against models pretrained on centralized public data and single-center private data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated FL protocol for video-based medical foundation models, enabling collaborative training without data sharing. It will provide empirical evidence on the trade-offs between federated and centralized pretraining for medical video. The resulting model and framework will be a step towards scalable, privacy-conscious AI for clinical applications.
proposals.novelty: While FL has been applied to medical imaging, its combination with self-supervised video pretraining for building a general-purpose medical vision foundation model is novel. This directly extends Yeung's recent video pretraining work into a distributed, privacy-preserving paradigm.

proposals.title: Bayesian Video Pretraining for Calibrated Uncertainty in 3D Medical Vision Tasks
proposals.motivation: Deep learning models for 3D medical image analysis, such as chest CT interpretation, are achieving high performance but lack reliable confidence estimates. This is a critical barrier to clinical adoption, as clinicians cannot trust predictions that are presented without a measure of uncertainty, especially for high-stakes diagnostic tasks. Current models are often overconfident, even when wrong, limiting their utility as assistive tools.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating scalable Bayesian deep learning methods, specifically Monte Carlo Dropout and Deep Ensembles, into a video-pretraining framework for 3D vision will yield models that provide well-calibrated, task-specific uncertainty estimates without sacrificing diagnostic performance on downstream chest CT tasks.
proposals.method: We will extend the video-pretraining paradigm, proven effective for 3D CT tasks in Yeung's prior work, by modifying the architecture and training to capture epistemic uncertainty. We will implement and compare two scalable Bayesian approaches: 1) a 3D ConvNet with Monte Carlo Dropout enabled during both pretraining on large-scale video datasets (e.g., Kinetics) and fine-tuning on chest CT data, and 2) a Deep Ensemble of models initialized from the same video-pretrained checkpoint. Uncertainty will be quantified as the predictive entropy and variance across stochastic forward passes or ensemble members. Calibration will be evaluated using Expected Calibration Error (ECE) on held-out medical datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, uncertainty-aware 3D medical vision model that provides reliable confidence scores alongside its predictions. It will establish a methodology for integrating Bayesian uncertainty quantification into large-scale pretraining pipelines for medical imaging. The resulting tool will be a significant step toward clinically trustworthy AI by allowing clinicians to identify and defer low-confidence cases.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian deep learning and medical image analysis exist separately, this proposal uniquely combines scalable Bayesian methods with the emerging, high-performance paradigm of video pretraining for 3D medical tasks. It moves beyond standard performance metrics to explicitly engineer and evaluate model trustworthiness through uncertainty within a pretrain-finetune framework, which is largely unexplored for 3D clinical applications pre-2026.

proposals.title: Multi-Task Action Recognition via Task-Conditioned Parameter Scaling and Cross-Task Rectification
proposals.motivation: Current action recognition models treat all action classes as a single classification task, which can lead to negative interference between semantically distinct or imbalanced classes. Multi-task learning (MTL) has shown success in other vision domains like medical imaging and scene understanding by learning shared representations while isolating task-specific features, but its structured application to fine-grained action recognition remains underexplored. This project aims to adapt advanced MTL paradigms to improve generalization and sample efficiency in video understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: Framing each action class as a separate, parameter-isolated task within a shared video backbone will reduce negative interference and improve overall recognition accuracy, particularly for rare or semantically similar actions. This benefit can be further amplified by using language-driven diagnosis to guide cross-task knowledge rectification.
proposals.method: We will adapt a transformer-based video architecture (e.g., TimeSformer or Video Swin) as a shared feature extractor. For each action class, we will attach a lightweight, task-specific adapter module, inspired by scaling methods like HyperNetworks or LoRA, to condition the shared parameters. Training will use a dynamically weighted loss across all tasks. Crucially, we will integrate a diagnosis phase using language models (e.g., CLIP text embeddings) to analyze confusion matrices, identifying pairs of tasks with high interference, and then apply targeted cross-task rectification via constrained fine-tuning or auxiliary contrastive objectives.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel MTL framework for action recognition that systematically manages inter-class relationships. We will contribute a new benchmark by re-framing existing datasets (e.g., Kinetics, AVA) in a multi-task setting and release code for our task-conditioned architecture. The findings will provide insights into the compositional structure of action spaces and more robust video models.
proposals.novelty: While MTL is known, its application to action recognition with each class as a separate task is novel, as is the combination of parameter-efficient task conditioning with a language-guided diagnostic step to explicitly mitigate cross-task interference learned from the data.

proposals.title: MedAlign: A Framework for Faithful Visual-Language Explanations in Medical Imaging via Counterfactual Pretraining
proposals.motivation: The deployment of high-performance black-box vision models in clinical settings is hindered by a lack of interpretability, which erodes clinician trust and poses a significant barrier to regulatory approval (e.g., FDA). Pre-2026, methods for explaining model decisions often produce post-hoc rationales that are not faithful to the model's actual reasoning, creating a dangerous gap between performance and reliable explanation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a vision-language model, co-pretrained on medical images and paired textual reports with explicit counterfactual augmentation, will generate more faithful and clinically plausible explanations for its diagnostic predictions than models using post-hoc attribution methods.
proposals.method: We will develop MedAlign, a transformer-based visual-language model. Building on VideoMAE-style masked autoencoder pretraining for robust 3D/2D medical image features, we will co-train on large-scale datasets (e.g., MIMIC-CXR-Rad) where image-text pairs are naturally aligned. The key novelty is a counterfactual data augmentation module that synthetically alters critical image regions (e.g., via inpainting) and generates corresponding 'negative' report text, forcing the model to learn a grounded, causal relationship between visual features and diagnostic language. Inference will jointly output a classification and a free-text explanation citing relevant visual findings.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a new pretraining paradigm integrating counterfactual reasoning for medical vision-language models, 2) a benchmark and metrics for evaluating the clinical plausibility and faithfulness of generated explanations against post-hoc methods, and 3) an open framework that moves beyond saliency maps towards auditable, text-based model reasoning.
proposals.novelty: While post-hoc explainability (e.g., Grad-CAM) and generic VLM pretraining exist, jointly learning diagnosis and faithful explanation generation through counterfactual medical image-text pretraining is novel. It directly addresses the clinical need for models whose explanations are intrinsic to their decision process.

proposals.title: A Framework for Task-Agnostic, Auditable Pretraining to Facilitate Regulatory Approval of Medical Computer Vision Models
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in medical computer vision, real-world clinical deployment is bottlenecked by stringent regulatory pathways (e.g., FDA 510(k)) which require extensive, task-specific validation and interpretability. Current large-scale pretrained models, while performant, are often 'black boxes' trained on non-medical or narrowly defined medical data, making their adaptation and audit for new clinical tasks legally and technically challenging.
proposals.hypothesis: A medical vision foundation model, pretrained on a large-scale, multi-modal dataset of paired chest CT volumes and radiology reports with a focus on generating human-interpretable, language-grounded feature representations, will enable more efficient regulatory submission by providing a standardized, auditable pretraining backbone that simplifies downstream task-specific validation.
proposals.method: We will scale the pretraining approach from 'Video Pretraining Advances 3D Deep Learning on Chest CT Tasks' by constructing a massive dataset of longitudinal chest CTs paired with their corresponding radiology text reports. We will use a dual-encoder architecture (a 3D CNN or Vision Transformer for CT, and a clinical BERT for text) trained with contrastive and masked language modeling objectives. Crucially, we will integrate methods from 'Diagnosing and Rectifying Vision Models using Language' to build an intrinsic audit trail, where model predictions for a pathology (e.g., a nodule) can be explicitly linked to relevant phrases in the original report and similar cases in the pretraining corpus.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a new large-scale pretrained model for 3D medical vision with built-in interpretability links, 2) a systematic framework for documenting the model's decision provenance to meet regulatory 'explainability' requirements, and 3) empirical evidence that such a framework reduces the data and computational burden for validating new downstream clinical tasks compared to training from scratch.
proposals.novelty: While medical foundation models exist, none are explicitly designed from the ground up with an audit trail for regulatory compliance, merging scalable pretraining with a language-grounded structure specifically for streamlining the approval process, not just final task performance.

proposals.title: Surgical Action Recognition via Language-Guided Adaptation of Video Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Automated analysis of surgical and clinical procedure videos can enable objective skill assessment, error detection, and workflow optimization. However, developing models from scratch is hindered by the scarcity of large-scale, annotated medical video datasets. Meanwhile, general-domain video foundation models (e.g., VideoMAE, TimeSformer), pretrained on massive web video collections, have shown remarkable transferability for action recognition but remain unexplored for the nuanced, structured tasks in clinical settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that large-scale video foundation models, when adapted using a language-guided rectification framework and targeted fine-tuning on a curated medical video dataset, will achieve strong performance on clinical action recognition tasks without requiring prohibitive amounts of medical-specific pretraining data.
proposals.method: We will adapt a VideoMAE-V2 or TimeSformer model, pretrained on Kinetics or Something-Something V2, to surgical video datasets (e.g., Cholec80, JIGSAWS). The core adaptation will use a two-stage approach: 1) A 'diagnosis' stage where a vision-language model (e.g., CLIP) generates textual descriptions of model failures on medical data, identifying domain shifts. 2) A 'rectification' stage where these textual insights guide targeted adversarial domain adaptation and the creation of a hybrid loss function combining standard action classification with a language-alignment loss on feature embeddings. Fine-tuning will leverage progressive scaling of the dataset size to evaluate data efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated framework for efficiently transferring general video models to the medical domain, significantly reducing the data requirements for clinical AI. It will also produce new benchmarks for surgical action recognition using adapted foundation models and insights into the cross-domain transferability of spatiotemporal representations.
proposals.novelty: While video pretraining is known, its application to clinical video is nascent. The novelty lies in explicitly using language as a diagnostic and rectification tool to guide the adaptation process, a method inspired by Yeung's prior work on diagnosing vision models, but now applied to the spatiotemporal domain for a critical medical task.

proposals.title: EdgeMed-ViT: Data-Efficient Distillation of Vision Transformers for Real-Time Point-of-Care Medical Imaging
proposals.motivation: The deployment of high-performance vision models on portable medical devices is hindered by their computational cost and reliance on cloud connectivity, limiting real-time diagnostics in resource-constrained settings. While scaling up models has driven progress, scaling down for efficient edge deployment without sacrificing medical task accuracy remains a critical, unsolved challenge. This work directly addresses the need for accessible, on-device AI in global health and point-of-care medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a task-aware, language-guided knowledge distillation framework, leveraging large-scale pretrained video models as teachers, can produce ultra-efficient student vision models that match the diagnostic performance of larger models on chest CT tasks when deployed on edge hardware.
proposals.method: We will adapt the 'Diagnosing and Rectifying Vision Models using Language' approach to create a distillation pipeline where natural language descriptions of pathologies guide the transfer of task-critical features. The teacher model will be a video-pretrained transformer (inspired by 'Video Pretraining Advances 3D Deep Learning on Chest CT Tasks'), and the student will be a meticulously scaled-down MobileViT or ConvNeXt variant. We will employ progressive layer pruning and neural architecture search optimized for low-power GPUs, validated on a curated dataset of chest CT slices and corresponding radiology reports.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a family of open-source, deployable vision models for thoracic abnormality detection, benchmarked on real edge devices. It will contribute a novel distillation methodology that uses language as a semantic filter for efficient knowledge transfer, moving beyond standard logit matching. The resulting models will demonstrate that high accuracy in 3D medical vision is achievable without prohibitive compute.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic combination of language-guided diagnosis for distillation targeting and the use of temporal-pretrained models as teachers for static 3D medical image tasks, a strategy not explored in prior edge-AI literature. This moves beyond simple architectural tweaks to a holistic, task-aware compression framework.

proposals.title: Cross-Modal Pretraining for Holistic Patient Phenotyping: Fusing Chest CT Volumes with Clinical Notes
proposals.motivation: Clinical decision-making relies on synthesizing information from disparate data modalities, such as medical imaging and electronic health record (EHR) text. Current AI models in healthcare are often siloed, analyzing one modality in isolation, which limits their ability to capture comprehensive patient states and can lead to suboptimal predictions. A scalable method to jointly learn from 3D medical images and unstructured clinical text is a critical unmet need for building robust diagnostic and prognostic tools.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a cross-modal pretraining framework, which aligns representations from 3D chest CT scans and their corresponding radiology reports, will learn more generalizable and clinically meaningful features than unimodal models, leading to superior performance on downstream tasks like disease classification and patient outcome prediction.
proposals.method: We will develop a dual-encoder architecture based on pre-2026 scaling principles. A 3D vision transformer (ViT), initialized from video-pretrained weights as in Yeung's prior work, will encode CT volumes. A clinical language model (e.g., a BioBERT variant) will encode de-identified radiology reports. During large-scale pretraining on paired CT-report datasets like MIMIC-CXR, we will employ contrastive learning (e.g., CLIP-style) and masked language modeling objectives to align the two modalities. The fused representations will then be fine-tuned on specific clinical prediction tasks using a task-specific fusion head.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a scalable, pretrained model for 3D vision-language fusion in clinical data, 2) a benchmark for evaluating cross-modal clinical understanding, and 3) empirical evidence on how joint representation learning improves model robustness and interpretability for complex patient analyses compared to late-fusion or unimodal baselines.
proposals.novelty: While multimodal fusion is known, scaling this to the joint pretraining of high-dimensional 3D medical images (CT) and long-form clinical text using contrastive objectives is largely unexplored. The approach moves beyond simple concatenation or late fusion to learn a unified, semantically aligned embedding space from the outset.

proposals.title: Diagnosing and Enhancing Medical Vision Models via Controllable, Pathology-Aware Synthetic Image Generation
proposals.motivation: The development of robust medical vision models is bottlenecked by the scarcity of large, diverse, and well-annotated datasets, which are expensive and ethically challenging to acquire. Synthetic data generation offers a solution, but current methods often produce unrealistic images or lack fine-grained control over pathological features, limiting their utility for rigorous model validation and targeted data augmentation.
proposals.hypothesis: By leveraging large-scale pretrained generative models and explicit language-driven control, we can synthesize highly realistic and controllable medical images that can effectively diagnose failure modes in existing vision models and, through targeted augmentation, rectify their performance on rare or complex pathologies.
proposals.method: We will build upon diffusion models (e.g., Stable Diffusion) pretrained on natural images and adapt them to the medical domain using a curated dataset of chest CT scans with paired textual radiology reports. A key innovation is the integration of a pathology-aware control module, using fine-grained language embeddings from clinical text to guide the synthesis of specific anatomical variations and disease patterns. We will employ a training regimen similar to Video Pretraining for 3D tasks, treating adjacent slices as temporal frames to ensure 3D consistency. The generated images will be used to create challenging test suites and for targeted adversarial training.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel framework for generating semantically controlled synthetic medical images, a new diagnostic benchmark for stress-testing model robustness, and a demonstrated methodology for using synthetic data to improve model performance on underrepresented conditions. It will advance the paradigm of using generative models not just for data expansion, but for model diagnosis and repair.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses GANs for medical image synthesis, our approach uniquely integrates large-scale pretrained diffusion models with explicit language guidance from clinical reports for fine-grained control, directly extending Yeung's 'Diagnosing and Rectifying' vision-language framework from natural to medical domains.

proposals.title: One-Shot Hyperbolic Prototypical Networks for Medical Image Classification
proposals.motivation: Few-shot learning is critical for medical imaging where labeled data is scarce, but existing methods still require multiple examples per class. Single-example (one-shot) learning remains a major challenge, especially for fine-grained anatomical distinctions. Hyperbolic embeddings, which better represent hierarchical relationships, offer a promising but underexplored geometry for this extreme data regime.
proposals.hypothesis: Leveraging hyperbolic space for prototype and feature embedding will enable more robust and accurate one-shot classification compared to standard Euclidean few-shot methods, by better preserving the hierarchical structure inherent in medical imaging data.
proposals.method: We will adapt Prototypical Networks to operate in the Poincaré ball model of hyperbolic space. A vision backbone (e.g., ResNet) pretrained on a large-scale medical image dataset (e.g., ChestX-ray14) will be used as a feature extractor. Prototypes for the support set (one image per class) will be computed via hyperbolic averaging in the tangent space. Query embeddings will be classified by their hyperbolic distance to these prototypes, with the entire system trained end-to-end using a hyperbolic softmax loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new benchmark for one-shot learning in medical vision by introducing a geometrically principled framework. It will provide a practical method for rapid adaptation of models to novel diagnostic tasks with minimal annotation, directly advancing the 'dataset' and 'performance' themes in low-data regimes.
proposals.novelty: While hyperbolic networks exist and few-shot learning is well-studied, their combination for the extreme case of one-shot learning, particularly applied to medical image analysis, is novel. This pushes 'scale down' by focusing on minimal data efficiency rather than model/data scaling.

proposals.title: EconomyNet: Monocular 3D Surgical Motion Estimation and Skill Assessment from Laparoscopic Video
proposals.motivation: Objective assessment of surgical skill, particularly tool motion economy, is crucial for training and credentialing but remains labor-intensive. Current automated methods often rely on stereo laparoscopes or external trackers, which are not ubiquitously available in clinical practice. A robust monocular solution would enable scalable skill analysis from the vast archives of existing single-view laparoscopic video.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model pre-trained on large-scale, multi-view video datasets can learn rich spatiotemporal priors, enabling accurate 3D tool motion estimation and economy scoring from a single 2D laparoscopic view without explicit depth supervision.
proposals.method: We will leverage VideoMAE V2 or a similar masked autoencoder for self-supervised pretraining on a large, diverse collection of surgical and non-surgical video data. This model will be fine-tuned on annotated monocular laparoscopic videos (e.g., from JIGSAWS or a new dataset) using a multi-task objective. The architecture will combine a temporal transformer backbone with a lightweight 3D pose regression head, supervised via a novel loss function that jointly optimizes for 2D keypoint reprojection accuracy and physically plausible 3D trajectory smoothness to infer depth and 3D motion.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a fully automated pipeline for 3D surgical motion analysis from ubiquitous monocular systems, lowering the barrier to widespread skill assessment. It will produce a novel benchmark dataset with monocular video and corresponding 3D tool motion annotations. The project will also advance foundational understanding of how pretraining transfers to the geometrically ambiguous domain of monocular endoscopy.
proposals.novelty: The approach challenges the established need for stereo or depth data in quantitative surgical analytics by directly inferring 3D kinematics from 2D video. It uniquely combines large-scale video pretraining—a technique proven in natural scenes but underexplored for this specific geometric task—with a physics-informed learning objective tailored for surgical tool motion.

ideas.title: Modality-Aligned Personalization via Final Model Ensembles
ideas.description: This research direction tackles personalization for clients with data from different modalities (e.g., text, audio, medical images) under strict privacy constraints. It uses unsupervised domain adaptation techniques on the client side to project each client's unique data distribution into a shared, aligned feature space. The server then personalizes a global model for a new client by intelligently ensembling or distilling from a collection of final, pre-trained models from other clients, using only their adapted feature representations as a guide. This achieves effective cross-modal personalization without ever sharing raw data, model gradients, or parameter updates, bridging the modality gap while preserving privacy.

ideas.title: Task-Aware Geometric Regularization for Optimal Modality Alignment
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a novel learning paradigm that integrates explicit geometric penalties (e.g., based on distances, angles, or manifold curvature from computational geometry) as a form of regularization, but critically ties the strength and form of this regularization to the empirical performance on a target fusion task. Instead of universally minimizing the modality gap, the method learns to shape the latent space geometry such that the induced alignment optimally facilitates the specific interactions required for tasks like VQA or cross-modal retrieval. This creates a feedback loop where task performance guides geometric regularization, and geometric constraints structure the latent space for efficient fusion, leading to models that achieve superior performance by learning a task-optimal alignment rather than a generic one.

ideas.title: Non-Convex Optimization, Modality Gaps, and the Emergence of Fairness Disparities
ideas.description: This research develops a theoretical and empirical framework to analyze how the non-convex optimization dynamics of deep neural networks influence the geometry of learned representations and, consequently, model fairness. It investigates the hypothesis that saddle points, flat minima, and other non-convex phenomena systematically affect the formation and magnitude of modality gaps between protected groups in multimodal or feature space. The goal is to derive formal conditions under which standard training procedures on non-convex losses provably lead to or exacerbate unfair disparities, moving beyond post-hoc fairness metrics to diagnose root causes in the training process itself. This enables the design of novel optimization-aware interventions that can steer learning toward more equitable solutions by directly targeting the problematic geometric structures in the loss landscape.

ideas.title: Adapting Geostatistical Kriging to Map Multimodal Embedding Distributions via Optimal Transport
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of using Optimal Transport (OT) to measure the modality gap by drawing an analogy from geostatistics. In geostatistics, the spatial correlation structure of sampled data is modeled with a variogram and used for optimal spatial interpolation (kriging). We adapt this solution to multimodal machine learning. First, we propose computing a 'transportogram' to model how the OT distance between sets of embeddings (e.g., image and text point clouds) relates to their semantic alignment or task performance, capturing the complex structure of the inter-modal gap. Second, we develop a kriging-inspired algorithm that uses this transportogram model to perform optimal 'interpolation' or mapping between modalities. For instance, given a new text embedding, the method would find the corresponding image embedding that minimizes the expected OT-based prediction error, effectively using the learned inter-modal structure to bridge the gap more intelligently than simple cosine distance or un-structured OT distance alone. This provides a principled, model-based framework for quantifying and actively navigating the modality gap.

ideas.title: Contrastive Learning with a Regularized Temperature Field
ideas.description: This approach learns a structured temperature field across the embedding space rather than a single global parameter. The temperature for a given anchor is predicted by a small network based on its representation and local neighborhood statistics, allowing for adaptive hardness adjustment per instance. The field is constrained by a regularization loss that prevents pathological values, ensuring stable training and avoiding representation collapse. This results in a more robust training dynamic that automatically calibrates the concentration of similarities based on the local structure of the data manifold.

ideas.title: Optimal Transport as a Dynamic Regularizer for Modality Gap Manipulation
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the concepts by using optimal transport (OT) distances not just as a static metric, but as a dynamic training signal to actively manipulate the modality gap. Instead of forcing full overlap, the OT distance—which accounts for the geometric structure of entire distributions—is used to regularize the alignment process, allowing the model to learn a task-informed, non-trivial alignment. This enables the discovery of an 'optimal gap,' a structured arrangement where modalities are aligned only where necessary for the task, preserving potentially useful modality-specific information elsewhere. The result is a framework that can theoretically improve downstream performance by learning more efficient and generalizable cross-modal representations than those from naive forced alignment or simple distance minimization.

ideas.title: Navigating Non-Convex Landscapes in Deep Learning via Topological Data Analysis
ideas.description: This research seed proposes extending theoretical and empirical analysis to modern deep neural networks with non-convex loss functions. Drawing an analogy from physical chemistry, specifically the study of protein folding energy landscapes, we adapt the solution of using topological data analysis (TDA). In protein folding, persistent homology is used to map the complex, non-convex landscape to identify critical features like funnels and barriers that guide folding pathways. Adapted to deep learning, the research idea involves applying TDA to characterize the topological structure of high-dimensional, non-convex loss landscapes. This would entail developing scalable methods to compute persistence diagrams or other topological summaries from loss surface samples during training. The goal is to empirically correlate these topological features with optimization dynamics (e.g., convergence speed, susceptibility to bad local minima) and generalization performance, thereby providing a novel theoretical and empirical framework for analyzing and potentially improving training in modern, complex deep learning models beyond the limitations of convex analysis.

ideas.title: Client-Adaptive Temperature for Cross-Silo Federated Contrastive Learning
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel federated contrastive learning method where the temperature parameter is not only learnable but is also personalized and adapted per client within a cross-silo setting. Each institutional client learns a local temperature that reflects the intrinsic hardness or semantic density of its own data distribution, which is then used to calibrate its local contrastive loss. These client-specific temperatures are shared and aggregated at the server to guide global model training, acting as lightweight, privacy-preserving signals of data heterogeneity. This approach enables the global model to learn more harmonized representations across diverse institutional data sources without sharing raw data, directly addressing statistical heterogeneity through a dynamically tuned learning objective.

ideas.title: Learnable Adaptive Geometric Priors for Modality Gap Control
ideas.description: This approach replaces fixed geometric penalties with a dynamic, data-informed regularizer. A small meta-network observes the statistics of the current joint embedding space (e.g., inter- and intra-modality distances) and predicts instance-specific geometric constraints. These adaptive constraints gently guide the representations toward a more uniform gap structure without forcing a pre-defined, potentially mismatched geometry. The system thus controls the modality gap by regularizing toward a learned, optimal geometry rather than a hand-designed one, preserving task-relevant information while improving alignment.

ideas.title: Dynamic Modality Overlap: Task-Adaptive Representation Geometry for Fusion
ideas.description: This research proposes that the optimal geometry for modality representations is not universally persistent nor universally eliminated, but is instead a learnable, task-dependent property. We will develop a model that dynamically adjusts the overlap and separation between modality embeddings based on a meta-objective for a target capability (e.g., compositional reasoning vs. dense retrieval). This allows the system to learn when to bridge the gap for tight fusion and when to maintain separation to preserve unique, complementary information, optimizing the representational structure for the end task rather than a generic alignment metric.

ideas.title: Personalizing Tiny Multi-Modal Models via Modality Gap Manipulation
ideas.description: This research investigates whether the modality alignment gap in extremely small-scale multi-modal models (e.g., <10M parameters) can be deliberately and predictably manipulated using only local, private data. The core hypothesis is that personalization can be achieved not by fine-tuning model weights, but by learning to apply a minimal, data-dependent transformation that reprojects the model's frozen latent spaces, effectively 'nudging' the modality gap for a specific user or context. This enables privacy-preserving personalization on edge devices, as only the final, personalized model (or a tiny transformation key) is shared or used, with no gradient updates ever exposed. The work thus unifies the study of fundamental multi-modal representation in constrained architectures with a novel, update-free personalization paradigm.

ideas.title: Bridge the Modality Gap via a Common Representational Substrate
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a modality gap is detrimental by proposing a solution inspired by cognitive neuroscience. The analogous problem of how the brain integrates disparate sensory inputs into a coherent concept is solved via a 'common code' or shared semantic substrate. Adapting this, the research idea is to explicitly construct a common representational substrate—a learned intermediate space—where features from different modalities are projected to encode unified, task-relevant semantics before fusion. The hypothesis is that bridging the gap via this engineered substrate will significantly improve performance on tasks requiring tight modality fusion, such as cross-modal retrieval or multimedia reasoning, compared to methods that attempt fusion across the original gap.

ideas.title: Critic-Guided Robust Aggregation: Distilling a Global Model from Personalized Ensembles
ideas.description: This system learns a robust global model from a federation of personalized clients by introducing a learned critic to safeguard the distillation process. The critic network, trained on a small curated validation set, evaluates the reliability and generalizability of each client model's behavior on representative anchor points. This evaluation then informs a selective, weighted knowledge distillation process where the global student model primarily learns from the most robust aspects of the personalized ensemble. The result is a global model that resists inheriting the flaws of its constituents, achieving superior generalization and adversarial robustness compared to direct aggregation methods.

ideas.title: Cross-Silo Federated Learning with Tiny Multi-Modal Models: Analyzing Modality Gaps in Resource-Constrained Institutional Settings
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the existence and behavior of the modality gap in federated learning environments that use extremely small-scale multi-modal models (e.g., for edge or IoT devices) across a few reliable institutional silos. It evaluates whether the data heterogeneity and communication patterns of cross-silo FL uniquely influence the alignment of visual and textual representations in highly capacity-constrained models, compared to large centralized models. The study aims to determine if the federated setting offers a regularizing effect that narrows the gap or if limited model capacity combined with non-IID data widens it, impacting downstream task performance. This reveals fundamental insights into the trade-offs between model size, data distribution, and multi-modal fusion in practical, privacy-preserving deployments.

ideas.title: Dynamic Semantic Alignment with Preserved Modality-Specific Nuances
ideas.description: This research investigates a hybrid architecture that challenges the necessity of a persistent, all-encompassing modality gap. Instead of forcing full overlap, the system learns to align modalities within a constrained, shared semantic subspace that captures cross-modal equivalences. Crucially, it maintains dedicated, non-overlapping pathways for high-dimensional, modality-unique features (e.g., visual texture, acoustic timbre). A gating mechanism, conditioned on the task, dynamically combines information from the aligned semantic core and the specific feature banks, enabling both robust cross-modal generalization and precise unimodal performance.

ideas.title: Diagnostic Domain Adaptation for Modality Gap Analysis in Diverse Multi-Modal Architectures
ideas.description: This research proposes a systematic study that applies domain adaptation techniques as a diagnostic instrument to investigate the modality gap phenomenon across a broad spectrum of multi-modal architectures, including encoder-decoder models, late-fusion transformers, and models with heavy cross-attention. By actively attempting to align the statistical distributions of different modalities within each architecture using methods like adversarial adaptation or maximum mean discrepancy minimization, the work will quantify the 'adaptability cost' or residual divergence as a concrete measure of the inherent modality gap. This approach moves beyond mere detection to provide a comparative analysis of how architectural choices fundamentally shape the alignment landscape between modalities, offering insights into which designs intrinsically mitigate or exacerbate the gap.

ideas.title: The Narrow Cone Effect: Diagnosing Its Impact and Systematically Engineering Networks for Improved Trainability
ideas.description: This research moves beyond simply altering initialization to ask a more fundamental question: is the initial narrow cone effect a genuine bottleneck for deep learning performance, or a benign byproduct? We design a comprehensive diagnostic suite to measure the effect's prevalence and strength across modern architectures. Subsequently, we engineer a unified 'trainability enhancement' protocol that intervenes at initialization, activation, and architecture levels, with the explicit goal of improving measurable outcomes like convergence reliability and robustness, not just altering the initial state. The final output is a principled understanding of when and how manipulating the initial optimization landscape leads to practical gains.

ideas.title: Solve the inverse problem: generate synthetic histology

ideas.title: Dynamic Latent Representation Learning for Evolving Client Personalization
ideas.description: This system abandons the traditional two-step cluster-then-personalize pipeline for a unified, adaptive framework. It employs a temporal deep learning model to maintain continuously updated, hierarchical latent embeddings for each client, capturing both stable traits and evolving states. Personalization policies are generated via a meta-learner that conditions on these dynamic embeddings, enabling real-time adaptation to individual changes and emergent population patterns. This ensures scalability to massive populations while preventing the staleness and misalignment inherent in fixed clustering.

ideas.title: Manifold-Aware Graph Federated Learning for Multi-Modal Client Networks
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a novel federated learning framework where the network of clients is modeled as a graph, and each client's data modality is understood as residing on a specific low-dimensional manifold within a shared representation space. A Graph Neural Network is then employed to propagate model updates across the client graph, but its message-passing functions are explicitly regularized or designed to respect the geometric distances and alignments between the inferred client manifolds. This approach allows the system to prioritize updates between clients with semantically similar data structures (nearby on a joint manifold) while mitigating interference from clients with divergent modalities, thereby improving convergence, personalization, and overall model performance in heterogeneous multi-modal federated environments.

ideas.title: Dynamic Multi-Modal Client Gap Analysis for Asynchronous Federated Learning
ideas.description: This research investigates the interplay between asynchronous client dynamics and multi-modal representation learning in federated settings. It aims to model how the performance and alignment gaps between three or more modalities (e.g., image, text, sensor) evolve when clients with different modality subsets and data distributions join, leave, or update at staggered intervals. The core innovation is developing adaptive client weighting and aggregation mechanisms that explicitly account for these time-varying, multi-modal gap dynamics to stabilize training and improve final model robustness across all modalities and participants.

ideas.title: Ensemble Consensus: Distilling a Global Model via Committee Voting from Personalized Clients
ideas.description: This idea adapts the concept of a consensus tree from phylogenetics to federated learning. The core problem is analogous: how to derive a single, robust representation (a global model / consensus tree) from a diverse set of individual representations (personalized client models / phylogenetic trees). The analogous solution uses a voting mechanism to identify and retain only the structural components that appear in a majority of the inputs. Adapted to this context, the research would develop methods to aggregate a collection of personalized client models not by averaging parameters, but by having them 'vote' on architectural or feature-level patterns. The distillation process would identify and lock in neurons, attention heads, or feature detectors that are consistently active across a super-majority of clients, while pruning or resetting inconsistent elements. The resulting global model would be a 'consensus model' that is inherently more robust and generalizable, as it is built from the common denominator of client-learned knowledge, effectively scaling the learning process by dualizing the aggregation objective from parameter space to structural consensus space.

ideas.title: Manifold-Aware Layer Composition for Cross-Modal Personalization
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a system where clients can personalize models by composing layers from diverse peer models, guided by a manifold analysis of the latent spaces. By characterizing the low-dimensional manifold structure of each layer's representations for different modalities, the system can predict which layer combinations will produce geometrically aligned and functionally coherent cross-modal behaviors. This moves beyond naive performance-based layer swapping to enable stable, personalized architectures that respect the intrinsic geometric constraints of the data, facilitating robust fusion of, for example, visual and textual capabilities from separate foundation models.

ideas.title: Quantifying and Resolving Manifold Entanglement for Robust Cross-Modal Gap Analysis
ideas.description: This approach first develops a diagnostic framework to measure the topological and geometric separability of modality-specific data structures within a shared embedding, moving beyond the assumption of clean manifold separation. Based on this diagnosis, it implements an adaptive manifold learning system that either disentangles the representations via constraints derived from the modality labels or, if the manifolds are fundamentally entangled, explicitly models their interaction zones and overlap. This results in a more robust and quantifiable understanding of cross-modal gaps, distinguishing between representational insufficiency and inherent geometric complexity.

ideas.title: Geometric Probing of Multi-Instance Final Models for Privacy-Preserving Personalization
ideas.description: This system enables robust personalization without sharing intermediate updates by having each user produce a small set of final models from strategically varied initializations near the global model. A server-side probe network is then trained to analyze the geometric constellation of these user models, learning to map the high-dimensional relationships between them into a personalized adaptation vector. This approach amplifies the user-specific signal from the noise of task-specific features, which is lost when comparing only two models. The method maintains privacy by communicating only final models and allows the probe network's knowledge to improve personalization for all users over time.

ideas.title: Joint Optimization of Personalized and Compressed Model Structures
ideas.description: This approach reframes the problem to avoid the conflict between compression and personalization by designing them together from the start. It introduces a training framework that learns a base model alongside a user-adaptive, highly efficient parameter delta, constrained to a predefined sparse or low-dimensional subspace. The system directly optimizes for a Pareto frontier of performance versus size/bitrate, ensuring compressed models retain personalized capabilities. This results in a single, efficient model artifact that delivers personalization without the overhead of separate full-sized components.

ideas.title: Forcing Shared Representational Space via Chemical Bonding Analogy
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a persistent modality gap is necessary by drawing an analogy from chemistry and materials science. The analogous problem is creating materials with emergent properties from constituent elements, solved by forcing atomic orbitals to hybridize into new, shared configurations (e.g., sp³ hybridization forming diamond's robust structure). The adapted research idea proposes designing neural architectures and training regimes that force modalities (like vision, language, audio) to form fundamentally new, hybrid 'representational orbitals' from early processing stages, rather than just aligning their separate representations in a late-fusion common space. The hypothesis is that this forced hybridization from the outset could yield more integrated, robust, and capability-rich multimodal representations, potentially improving tasks requiring deep cross-modal reasoning or synthesis.

ideas.title: Adapt masked autoencoding for 3D medical volumes

ideas.title: Task-Specific Tuning of Modality Gap via Optimal Control Principles
ideas.description: This idea adapts the concept of null-space optimization from control theory and robotics to the problem of determining the ideal modality gap for different downstream tasks. In robotics, a manipulator's redundant degrees of freedom (its null space) are used to optimize for secondary criteria without interfering with the primary end-effector control. Analogously, in a multimodal embedding space after contrastive alignment (e.g., CLIP), the precise distance or geometric relationship between matched image-text pairs (the modality gap) may represent a controllable degree of freedom that is not fully constrained by the primary zero-shot classification objective. The research would involve: 1) Formally defining the constraint manifold imposed by a baseline aligned model's primary task performance. 2) Modeling various downstream tasks (e.g., cross-modal retrieval, compositional reasoning, conditional generation) as secondary cost functions. 3) Developing an optimization framework to project these secondary objectives into the 'null space' of the primary task to solve for the task-specific optimal gap distance or geometric configuration. This provides a principled, control-theoretic method to tune the modality gap systematically, moving beyond empirical search for different applications.

ideas.title: Quantifying and Exploiting Loss Landscape Connectivity in Overparameterized Non-Convex Models
ideas.description: This research provides a rigorous, focused investigation into why gradient-based methods succeed in specific, prevalent non-convex settings. Theoretically, it develops measures of connectivity between low-loss regions in overparameterized networks, moving beyond point-wise critical point analysis. Empirically, it designs experiments to trace optimization paths and map connected manifolds of solutions for standard architectures and datasets. The outcome is a set of practical conditions under which efficient optimization is guaranteed, along with diagnostics to predict training difficulty for novel model designs.

ideas.title: Adapting Epidemic Spreading Models for Federated Learning via Graph Neural Networks
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from computational epidemiology. The analogous problem is modeling how a disease (or information) spreads through a structured contact network. Epidemiologists solve this using graph-based compartmental models that account for node states and connection strengths to simulate and control propagation. Adapted to the client relations context, we propose modeling the federated learning system as a contact graph where clients are nodes and their relationships (communication frequency, data distribution similarity, trust) are edges. A Graph Neural Network is used not just to propagate updates, but to learn the underlying 'spreading dynamics' of model information, effectively predicting the most influential pathways for update flow. This enables a data-driven scheduling and aggregation strategy that prioritizes updates through high-influence subgraphs, leading to more efficient and robust federated training, analogous to targeted interventions in an epidemic network.

ideas.title: Capacity Thresholds for Emergent Modality Alignment: Diagnosing the Multi-Modal Gap in Tiny Models
ideas.description: This research systematically investigates whether and under what conditions the modality alignment gap emerges in extremely small multi-modal models. It moves beyond a simple existence check by constructing a scaling law experiment that varies model capacity (parameters, layers, attention heads) and training data scale to identify critical thresholds for gap formation. The study employs controlled comparisons against uni-modal models of equivalent capacity to isolate the multi-modal alignment cost from general underfitting. The outcome will provide a diagnostic framework to predict alignment failure in constrained settings and inform efficient tiny multi-modal model design.

ideas.title: Adversarially-Robust Dynamic Relation Inference for Federated Learning with GNNs
ideas.description: This system addresses graph manipulation by not relying on a pre-declared or static client graph. Instead, it dynamically constructs a multi-layered similarity graph at each training round by comparing client model updates, local loss landscapes, and data distribution signatures. This inferred graph feeds into a robust GNN aggregator that employs attention mechanisms and spectral analysis to detect and isolate anomalous subgraph structures indicative of collusion or poisoning. The final aggregation weights updates based on both the learned node embeddings and a trust score derived from graph consistency over time, creating a resilient relational federated learning framework.

ideas.title: Manifold Topology of the Modality Gap in Tiny Multi-Modal Models
ideas.description: This research investigates the geometric structure of the modality gap in extremely small-scale multi-modal models (e.g., toy models with <1M parameters) using manifold learning techniques. By constructing and analyzing these minimal models, we can systematically vary architectural components (e.g., fusion mechanisms, embedding dimensions) and training regimes to observe their causal impact on whether modalities form separate, connected, or interleaved low-dimensional manifolds. This controlled approach isolates the fundamental mechanisms that generate the gap, distinguishing them from emergent large-scale phenomena. The resulting insights provide a foundational geometric theory for modality alignment, offering predictive design principles for building more efficient and integrated multi-modal systems from the ground up.

ideas.title: Use Isoperimetric Regularization to Shape the Modality Gap
ideas.description: The original idea proposes using geometric penalties from computational geometry to shape the modality gap. Drawing an analogy from statistical physics and materials science, specifically the modeling of phase separation via the Cahn-Hilliard equation, where a surface tension term penalizes excessive interface area to produce regular phase boundaries. The adapted solution is to introduce an isoperimetric-style regularization term in the multimodal learning objective. This term would act as a 'surface tension' for the modality gap in the latent space, penalizing the complexity (e.g., perimeter, curvature) of the boundary or manifold separating embeddings from different modalities. By minimizing this geometric penalty alongside standard alignment losses, the model is guided toward forming a more compact and regularized inter-modality gap, which can enhance cross-modal retrieval robustness and reduce overfitting to complex, arbitrary separations.

ideas.title: Manifold-Aware Influence Functions for Cross-Modal Client Benefit Estimation
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using influence functions to estimate how much a client in a federated or multi-modal system benefits from another's data, but with a crucial geometric refinement. First, manifold learning techniques (e.g., diffusion maps, UMAP) are applied to analyze the structure of the learned shared representation space, identifying separate low-dimensional manifolds for each data modality. Influence scores are then computed not in the ambient space, but with respect to distances and paths constrained to these manifolds. This allows the method to distinguish between 'within-manifold' influence, which refines a model for a known modality, and 'cross-manifold' influence, which helps bridge the gap to a new modality, providing a geometrically grounded measure of transferability and collaborative utility.

ideas.title: Causal Intervention Framework for Diagnosing and Reducing Multimodal Alignment Gaps
ideas.description: This research moves beyond descriptive gap analysis to establish a causal intervention framework for models with three or more modalities. It systematically perturbs architectural fusion points, training dynamics, and input data distributions to isolate the factors that cause, amplify, or reduce cross-modal alignment gaps. The methodology employs controlled ablation studies across diverse modality sets (e.g., text, image, audio; text, video, depth) to distinguish between fundamental representational mismatches and tractable optimization failures. The outcome is a diagnostic toolkit and a set of principled interventions for actively minimizing multimodal gaps during model development.

ideas.title: Activation-Aligned Mesh for Coherent Cross-Model Layer Composition
ideas.description: This system enables robust personalization by combining layers from different peer-trained models. It overcomes the fundamental incompatibility of naive stitching by inserting a trainable, low-rank Adapter Mesh between connected layers that projects activations into a compatible intermediate space. A fast compatibility pre-screening step evaluates potential layer pairs using a spectral analysis of their activation covariance, filtering out fundamentally misaligned combinations. The final, lightly trained mesh ensures the composite model behaves as a coherent single network, making cross-model personalization practically viable.

ideas.title: Robust, Assumption-Aware Client Benefit Estimation via Counterfactual Simulation and Uncertainty Calibration
ideas.description: This system provides a reliable measure of how much one client benefits from another's data in collaborative learning, without relying on the unstable assumptions of classical influence functions. It estimates benefit by comparing model performance for a client with and without another's data, using efficient amortized shadow model techniques and targeted retraining to simulate counterfactuals. A key innovation is an integrated validation module that explicitly quantifies the uncertainty and potential bias of each benefit estimate, flagging unreliable predictions. This creates a robust, assumption-aware framework that works for non-convex models, non-differentiable losses, and provides trustworthy estimates where prior methods would fail silently.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Multi-Scale Personalization via Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the hierarchical, multi-scale solution from ecology and biogeography to the problem of scaling personalization for massive client populations. The analogous solution involves modeling species distributions by first identifying broad biomes (macro-clusters) and then finer-scale ecological niches within them. Adapted to the research context, we first cluster clients into high-level 'behavioral biomes' based on stable, macro-scale features. Within each biome, a second clustering stage identifies finer-grained 'personalization niches' based on dynamic, micro-scale behavior. Personalization models are then trained to be specialized within niches but can share parameters or be meta-learned across similar niches in different biomes, creating a scalable, dual-level structure for efficient model training and deployment across millions of users.

ideas.title: Apply Geologic Layer Analysis to Modality Manifold Separation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics to the problem of analyzing if modalities lie on separate low-dimensional manifolds. In seismic stratigraphy, distinct geological layers are identified by analyzing discontinuities (unconformities) in seismic data. Analogously, we propose to treat the shared multimodal embedding space as a volumetric field. Instead of just identifying manifolds, we will apply manifold learning and topological data analysis techniques to explicitly map and characterize the structure of the gaps *between* modality manifolds. This involves quantifying local density gradients, connectivity, and separation to distinguish regions belonging to a coherent, modality-specific manifold from those constituting the inter-modal 'unconformities,' thereby providing a detailed structural understanding of the separation and potential bridges between modalities.

ideas.title: Bridging the Modality Gap via Cross-Domain Alignment Metrics from Materials Science
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to investigate the modality gap phenomenon in multi-modal architectures beyond dual-encoder contrastive frameworks. Drawing an analogy from materials science, where a 'property gap' between composite constituents is solved via standardized interfacial characterization techniques, we adapt this solution. The adapted idea is to create a diagnostic toolkit of standardized, architecture-agnostic metrics to quantify the alignment and separation between modalities in any multi-model system's latent representation. This would allow for systematic comparison of the gap's characteristics across diverse architectures (e.g., encoder-decoder, transformer-based fusion models), transforming the question from 'does a gap exist?' to 'what is the nature of the gap?'. The resulting diagnostics could then guide the development of targeted architectural modifications or novel loss functions to better bridge modalities, analogous to how materials scientists design coupling agents based on interfacial test results.

ideas.title: Manipulation-Robust Personalization via Costly-to-Fake, Multi-Signal Client Assessment
ideas.description: This research designs learning systems that achieve robust personalization with strategic clients by moving beyond single-source trust signals. The core innovation is a dynamic assessment mechanism that evaluates clients based on a portfolio of orthogonal, costly-to-fake signals related to data quality, update consistency, and cross-client utility. The system employs meta-learning to adaptively weight these signals, detecting and countering manipulation patterns. This creates a sustainable equilibrium where providing genuinely useful data is the most efficient strategy for clients to receive high-quality personalization, even under strong incentives to deceive.

ideas.title: Confidence-Aware Adaptive Computation Splitting for Severely Constrained Clients
ideas.description: This system equips severely resource-constrained devices with a two-tier inference strategy. A microscopic on-device model handles routine, high-certainty inferences directly and efficiently. For inputs where the local model's confidence is low, the system automatically identifies and delegates only the ambiguous computational subtask to a designated helper node (like a neighboring device or micro-server), then reintegrates the result. This approach maintains a baseline of functionality under total isolation while dynamically accessing stronger capabilities as needed, preserving utility without a fixed local resource footprint.

ideas.title: Immunological Negative Selection for Federated Clients
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'negative selection' algorithm from computational immunology to detect clients harming a federated learning system. In immunology, T-cells are screened to eliminate those that react to the body's own 'self' patterns, leaving only detectors for foreign 'non-self' threats. Similarly, we define the aggregate, beneficial contribution patterns of trusted clients as the federation's 'self'. We then algorithmically generate a set of detection rules or models that are validated to be non-reactive to this 'self' profile but become activated by anomalous contribution patterns in the benefit matrix. These deployed 'detectors' continuously monitor client updates, isolating those exhibiting 'non-self' signatures indicative of malicious intent (e.g., data poisoning, model manipulation) or severely low-quality data, thereby protecting the federation's integrity through a bio-inspired screening mechanism.
